<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Mean Time Between Failures" (MTBF) in the context of a hydraulic robot. How is it related to the failure rate $\lambda$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MTBF is the expected (mean) operating time of a robot between two consecutive failures. It is the reciprocal of the constant failure rate during the useful-life phase:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

For a hydraulic robot, if $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour, MTBF = 2000 hours.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark numerical illustration or interpretation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between Reliability and Availability. Why is availability more meaningful for repairable industrial robots?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reliability** $R(t)$ is the probability the system performs its function without failure under stated conditions for a specified time interval.
- **Availability** $A$ is the fraction of time the system is operationally ready, considering both uptime and repair downtime:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

For repairable industrial robots, availability is more meaningful because the system *will* be repaired after failure, and operations managers care about *actual uptime* rather than just failure probability. A robot with low reliability can still have high availability if MTTR is very small.

*(Award: 1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for the practical justification.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **bath-tub curve** for robot components. Describe each of the three phases and identify what type of failure distribution applies in the useful-life region. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 6-axis **electric robot** has the following component failure rates:
- Base servo motor: $\lambda_1 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$/hr
- Shoulder motor: $\lambda_2 = 7 \times 10^{-5}$/hr
- Elbow motor: $\lambda_3 = 8 \times 10^{-5}$/hr
- Wrist motors (3 identical): each $\lambda_4 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$/hr
- Controller: $\lambda_5 = 4 \times 10^{-5}$/hr

All components are arranged in series. Compute:
(i) The equivalent failure rate of the robot.
(ii) The system MTBF.
(iii) The reliability at $t = 2000$ hours.
(iv) The mission reliability for a 6-hour production shift. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Bath-Tub Curve Explanation (7 marks)

The **bath-tub curve** plots failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time $t$ and has three distinct regions:

**Phase 1 — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate *decreases* with time. Caused by manufacturing defects, improper installation, sub-standard components, debugging errors. Early-life failures are dominant. Reliability is modeled by **Weibull with β < 1**.

**Phase 2 — Useful Life:** Failure rate is approximately **constant**. Random failures occur due to external shocks, overloading, environmental stresses. This is the design operating region. Reliability follows the **exponential distribution**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Phase 3 — Wear-out:** Failure rate *increases* with time. Caused by mechanical wear, fatigue, corrosion, seal degradation. Reliability follows **Weibull with β > 1**.

KTU applications: Robot manufacturers perform **burn-in testing** to eliminate Phase 1 failures before shipping, and schedule preventive maintenance to replace components *before* Phase 3 begins.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks Phase 1, 2 marks Phase 2, 2 marks Phase 3, 1 mark engineering application.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step (i): Equivalent failure rate**

The robot has 6 motors (1 base + 1 shoulder + 1 elbow + 3 wrist) + 1 controller = 7 components in series.

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + 3\lambda_4 + \lambda_5$$

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = (6 + 7 + 8 + 15 + 4) \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = 40 \times 10^{-5} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step (ii): System MTBF**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 2500 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (iii): Reliability at 2000 hours**

$$R(2000) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} \cdot 2000} = e^{-(4.0 \times 10^{-4})(2000)} = e^{-0.8}$$

$$R(2000) = 0.4493 \approx 44.93\%$$

**Step (iv): Mission reliability for 6 hours**

$$R(6) = e^{-(4.0 \times 10^{-4})(6)} = e^{-0.0024}$$

Using $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for very small $x$:

$$R(6) \approx 1 - 0.0024 = 0.9976 \approx 99.76\%$$

**Conclusion:** The robot is highly reliable for short shifts (99.76% per 6 hours) but requires preventive maintenance long before 2000 hours of continuous operation.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark equivalent λ, 1 mark MTBF, 2 marks R(2000), 2 marks mission reliability, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare the **reliability characteristics** of hydraulic and electric robots. Discuss the dominant failure modes, typical MTBF ranges, and the effect of environmental factors. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hydraulic robot has **two pumps in parallel redundancy**. Each pump has failure rate $\lambda = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ failures/hour. Compute:
(i) Reliability of a single pump at $t = 100$ hours.
(ii) System reliability at $t = 100$ hours.
(iii) System MTBF for the parallel configuration.
(iv) Availability if MTTR per pump = 4 hours. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison of Hydraulic vs Electric Reliability (7 marks)

| Aspect | Hydraulic Robot | Electric Robot |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Typical MTBF | 5,000–15,000 hours | 20,000–60,000 hours |
| Dominant failure | Seal leaks, pump wear, valve sticking | Motor winding burn-out, encoder drift, drive electronics |
| Failure distribution | Often Weibull ($\beta > 1$) | Near-exponential |
| Environmental sensitivity | High (temperature affects viscosity, contamination causes failure) | Moderate (electronics more robust if sealed) |
| Maintenance | Frequent (fluid replacement, seal inspection) | Less frequent (modular replacement) |
| Power-to-weight ratio | High | Moderate |
| Best suited for | Heavy payloads, harsh environments | Precision tasks, clean rooms |

Hydraulic robots are favored for **high-force, heavy-payload** applications (forging, die casting, mining), while electric robots dominate **precision, assembly, and cleanroom** environments.

*(Mark distribution: 4 marks for comparison table/content, 3 marks for engineering discussion.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step (i): Single pump reliability at 100 hours**

$$R_1(100) = e^{-(2.0 \times 10^{-3})(100)} = e^{-0.20} = 0.8187$$

**Step (ii): Parallel system reliability at 100 hours**

For two identical pumps in parallel:

$$R_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - R_1)^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.8187)^2$$

$$R_{\text{system}} = 1 - (0.1813)^2 = 1 - 0.03287 = 0.9671$$

**Step (iii): System MTBF (parallel configuration)**

For two equal parallel components with constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{3}{2 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 750 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (iv): Availability calculation**

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{parallel}} = 750 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTTR} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{750}{750 + 4} = \frac{750}{754}$$

$$A = 0.9947 = 99.47\%$$

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks step (i), 2 marks step (ii), 1 mark step (iii), 2 marks step (iv).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common mistakes that cost marks every semester:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing MTBF and MTTF** — MTBF applies to *repairable* systems; MTTF to *non-repairable* ones. Robotic systems are repairable, so MTBF is used.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply parallel pump failure rates by count** — In Question B, three wrist motors means $3\lambda_4$, not $\lambda_4$.
> 
> 3. **Misapplying the parallel formula** — Students often write $R_{\text{parallel}} = R_1 + R_2$ which is WRONG. The correct formula is $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$.
> 
> 4. **Not stating units** — MTBF must be quoted in **hours**. Failure rate in **failures/hour**.
> 
> 5. **Using linear approximations blindly** — $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ is valid only for $x < 0.1$. For $x \geq 0.1$, you must compute the actual exponential.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring the parallel MTBF derivation** — Many students write MTBF$_{\text{parallel}} = 1/\lambda$ which is the *single-component* MTBF, not the parallel system MTBF.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reliability** is a probability between 0 and 1, always stated with a time window.
- **Exponential distribution** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ applies during the useful-life (constant failure rate) phase — default assumption in KTU problems.
- **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$ for constant failure rate.
- **Weibull distribution** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ applies for infant-mortality ($\beta < 1$) and wear-out ($\beta > 1$) phases.
- **Series system:** $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod R_i$ and $\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum \lambda_i$ — adding components *reduces* reliability.
- **Parallel (redundant) system:** $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ — redundancy *improves* reliability.
- **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ — accounts for repair downtime.
- **Bath-tub curve** has three phases: infant mortality, useful life, wear-out.
- **Electric robots** generally have **higher reliability and MTBF** than hydraulic robots, but lower power density.
- **Hydraulic robot dominant failures:** seal leaks, pump wear, valve sticking, fluid contamination.
- **Electric robot dominant failures:** motor winding burn-out, encoder drift, drive electronics failure.
- **Mission reliability** for short-duration shifts uses $R(t) \approx 1 - \lambda t$ for $t$ small.
- **Always quote units** — MTBF in hours, $\lambda$ in failures/hour.
- **Independent components assumption** is mandatory when applying series/parallel formulas.
- **Series vs Parallel:** Most robot architectures are series; safety-critical subsystems (dual encoder, redundant pump) are parallel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roboethics and Levels of Robomorality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT417 - ROBOT SAFETY AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Roboethics and Levels of Robomorality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Roboethics — The Formal Definition

**Roboethics** is a branch of applied ethics that studies the moral behaviour, ethical conduct, design philosophy, and regulatory framework governing the design, manufacture, deployment, and operation of intelligent robotic systems, including the consequences of their interaction with humans.

The term **"Roboethics"** was formally coined by **Gianmarco Veruggio** at the **First International Symposium on Roboethics (2004)** held in Sanremo, Italy. It is officially endorsed as a sub-discipline of ethics by the IEEE Robotics and Automation Society.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 (PERAT417, Module 3):**
> Roboethics is not the same as "robot morality." Roboethics is a **human discipline** that regulates *how humans design, build, and use* robots. Robot morality (Robomorality) refers to *the ethical behaviour embedded in or reasoned by the robot itself*.

## 1.2 Robomorality — The Formal Definition

**Robomorality** (also called **Machine Morality** or **Artificial Morality**) refers to the capacity of a robotic or autonomous system to **perceive, evaluate, and act upon ethical principles** during the performance of its tasks — without (or with minimal) direct human moral supervision.

Robomorality is built progressively through increasing layers of ethical autonomy:

- **Ethical Awareness** — The robot recognises that an action may have moral consequences.
- **Ethical Reasoning** — The robot evaluates actions using embedded or learned ethical frameworks.
- **Ethical Action** — The robot executes or refuses an action consistent with its ethical conclusions.
- **Ethical Accountability** — The robot can justify or be questioned about its moral decisions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Moral Compass Inside a Machine**
> Think of Robomorality like the **automatic braking system (ABS)** in modern cars.
> - In **older cars (Level 0)**, the driver alone was responsible for braking — no system intervened. The car itself had no ethical awareness, but its behaviour still had safety (ethical) consequences.
> - In **modern cars (Level 1–2)**, sensors detect obstacles and the car automatically applies brakes. The system has **embedded ethical rules** — "do not hit a pedestrian."
> - In **future autonomous cars (Level 3+)**, the vehicle might *reason*: "If I cannot brake in time, should I swerve into a wall to save a pedestrian?" That is **explicit ethical reasoning**.
>
> Just as ABS evolved from a passive mechanical concept into an intelligent safety feature, Robomorality evolves from *no built-in ethics* → *embedded rules* → *autonomous ethical reasoning*.

## 1.4 Two Fundamental Branches of Roboethics (Veruggio's Classification)

| Branch | Focus | Stakeholder |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| **Human-Robot Interaction Ethics** (Outer) | How humans *use* robots responsibly | Humans (operators, users, society) |
| **Machine Ethics / Robomorality** (Inner) | How robots *behave* ethically | Robots / Autonomous Systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction to Remember for the Exam:**
> - **Roboethics** = Ethics *about* robots (a human responsibility).
> - **Robomorality** = Ethics *by* robots (a machine capability).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Levels of Robomorality?

As robots migrate from **structured industrial cages** (where they perform repetitive, deterministic tasks) into **unstructured human environments** (homes, hospitals, roads, battlefields), the question shifts from:

> *"Can the robot do the task?"* → *"Should the robot do the task, and on what moral grounds?"*

This transition demands a **graduated classification** of moral capability. The most cited frameworks in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **James H. Moor's Four Levels** (2006) — *"The Nature, Importance, and Difficulty of Machine Ethics"*.
2. **Wendell Wallach & Colin Allen's Spectrum** (2009) — *"Moral Machines: Teaching Robots Right from Wrong"*.
3. **Gianmarco Veruggio's Three-Level Roboethics Framework** (2004) — Operational, Programmed, Autonomous.

## 2.2 Framework 1 — James H. Moor's Levels (Most Frequently Asked)

James Moor, a philosopher at Dartmouth College, classified intelligent machines into **four logical levels** based on their ethical agency. This is a **descriptive (not normative)** framework — it describes *what is*, not *what should be*.

### Level 0 — Ethical Impact Agents
- Robots have **no ethical sensitivity** at all.
- Their actions *do* affect humans ethically (positively or negatively), but the system is unaware of these consequences.
- **Moral responsibility lies entirely with the designer, manufacturer, and operator.**
- **Example:** An industrial robotic arm in an automotive assembly line. It performs spot welding with no awareness that a misplaced weld could endanger a worker downstream.

### Level 1 — Implicit Ethical Agents
- Robots are designed with **constraints and safety rules** built into their architecture so that they inherently *avoid* unethical outcomes.
- The ethics is **implicit** — embedded in hardware/software guards, but not reasoned about.
- **Example:** A surgical robot (e.g., Da Vinci) with a software-defined "no-go zone" that prevents the end-effector from entering a dangerous anatomical region. The robot does not "know" why, but it cannot violate the rule.

### Level 2 — Explicit Ethical Agents
- Robots can **identify and represent ethical features** of situations (e.g., harm, fairness, consent).
- They use formal representations (often **deontic logic**, **consequentialist scoring**, or **case-based reasoning**) to evaluate actions.
- The robot *reasons* about right and wrong, but does not bear moral responsibility itself.
- **Example:** An autonomous vehicle that, when faced with an unavoidable collision, calculates the *expected utility* for each action (swerve vs. brake vs. continue) and selects the one that minimises total harm.

### Level 3 — Full Ethical Agents
- Robots possess **consciousness, free will, and intentionality** — the philosophical prerequisites for moral agency.
- They can be held **morally responsible** for their actions.
- **Status:** Currently **theoretical / speculative**. No machine has reached this level. This is the focus of intense philosophical debate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Moor's Core Argument:** Levels 0 and 1 are *engineering* problems (constraint design). Levels 2 and 3 are *philosophy + AI* problems (reasoning + consciousness).

## 2.3 Framework 2 — Wallach & Allen's Spectrum (Operative vs. Full Moral Agents)

Wendell Wallach (Yale) and Colin Allen (Indiana) proposed a **two-dimensional spectrum** rather than discrete levels:

| Dimension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Moral Top-Down (Operative Morality)** | Rules, principles, and constraints *programmed in* by the designer (e.g., Asimov's Three Laws). |
| **Moral Bottom-Up (Autonomous Morality)** | The system *learns* ethical behaviour from data, experience, or reinforcement (e.g., inverse reinforcement learning from human demonstrations). |

Their **three classes** of machine morality are:

1. **Ethical Impact Systems** — Same as Moor's Level 0.
2. **Ethically Operative Systems** — Robots with explicit built-in ethical controls (a hybrid of Moor's Level 1 and Level 2).
3. **Ethically Autonomous Systems** — Robots capable of sensing, evaluating, and acting on ethical principles without human intervention.

## 2.4 Framework 3 — Veruggio's Three Operational Levels of Roboethics

| Level | Name | Description | Example |
|-------|------|-------------|---------|
| **L1** | **Operational Ethics** | Ethics applied *by humans* during design, deployment, and use. The robot itself has no moral awareness. | A factory robot's safety cage — engineered by humans following IEC 61508. |
| **L2** | **Programmed Ethics** | Ethics *embedded into* the robot via rules, constraints, or learned policies. The robot follows ethics but does not reason about them. | A collaborative robot (cobot) that stops instantly upon human contact (ISO/TS 15066 power-and-force limiting). |
| **L3** | **Autonomous Moral Reasoning** | The robot *evaluates* ethical dilemmas, weighs conflicting principles, and decides independently. | A self-driving car in a no-win accident scenario selecting the minimum-harm path. |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarises every framework you must memorise for the KTU 2024 PERAT417 examination. No pipes are used inside cells — only `\vert` — to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| Framework | Author(s) & Year | Number of Levels | Core Question Addressed | Highest Level (Current Reality) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Moor's Four Levels** | James H. Moor, **2006** | **4** (Impact → Implicit → Explicit → Full) | "How ethically aware is the machine?" | **Level 2** (Explicit Ethical Agents) — partially realised |
| **Wallach–Allen Spectrum** | Wallach & Allen, **2009** | **3** classes (Impact → Operative → Autonomous) | "Is morality built-in or learned?" | **Ethically Operative** systems — commercial reality |
| **Veruggio's Roboethics Levels** | Gianmarco Veruggio, **2004** | **3** (Operational → Programmed → Autonomous Reasoning) | "Who is the moral agent — human or robot?" | **L2** — standard in industrial cobots |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Robotics** → Veruggio L2 ethics prevent surgical robots from harming patients.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** → Moor's Level 2 reasoning is needed for unavoidable-accident decisions.
- **Defence Robotics** → Asimov-style rules (Wallach–Allen Top-Down) govern weapons systems; bottom-up learning is **forbidden** under current UN CCW protocols.
- **Industrial Cobots** → Veruggio L2 power-and-force-limiting follows ISO/TS 15066.
- **Affective / Companion Robots** → Moor Level 1 implicit ethics prevents emotional manipulation of vulnerable users (e.g., elderly, children).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Summary:**
> Levels of Robomorality are **not philosophical luxuries**. They directly determine:
> 1. Which **ISO standard** applies (ISO 13482 for personal care, ISO 10218 for industrial, ISO/TS 15066 for collaborative).
> 2. Which **risk assessment methodology** must be used (TS-B for human-robot coexistence).
> 3. Which **liability framework** governs accidents (manufacturer, operator, or machine).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation of Moral Decision Logic

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Frameworks Side-by-Side

This is the **single most important comparative table** KTU examiners expect for 14-mark questions.

| Criterion | Moor's Framework | Wallach–Allen Framework | Veruggio's Framework |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Origin Year** | 2006 | 2009 | 2004 |
| **Structure** | Discrete 4 levels | 3 classes + 2D spectrum | 3 operational levels |
| **Moral Responsibility Located At** | Lower levels: humans; Higher levels: machines | Designed-in (top-down) or learned (bottom-up) | Levels 1–2: humans; Level 3: machine |
| **Type of Ethics** | Descriptive (what exists) | Prescriptive (what to design) | Operational (what to deploy) |
| **Considers Consciousness?** | Yes (Level 3 requires it) | No (focuses on behaviour, not inner states) | No (focuses on behaviour) |
| **Direct Standardisation Link** | Indirect (philosophical foundation for AI ethics) | Direct (informs Asimov-inspired rule sets) | Direct (maps to ISO/TS 15066, IEC 61508) |
| **Maturity in Industry** | Conceptual | Partially operationalised in autonomous vehicles | Fully operationalised in collaborative robots |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of an Autonomous Moral Decision (Moor Level 2)

For an autonomous agent to operate as an **Explicit Ethical Agent**, the following **logical pipeline** must be implemented. Each step is derived from Moor's philosophical framework.

### Step 1 — Define the Ethical Ontology

The system must encode moral categories as machine-readable variables.

$$E = \left\{ \text{harm}, \text{fairness}, \text{consent}, \text{truth}, \text{autonomy}, \text{privacy} \right\}$$

**Explanation:** This is the *vocabulary of ethics* the agent understands. Without an ontology, the agent cannot identify ethical features in a situation.

### Step 2 — Sense and Classify the Ethical State

The agent must map raw sensor data into ethical categories from $E$.

$$s(t) = \mathcal{F}_{\text{perceive}}\bigl(\,I(t)\,\bigr)$$

where $I(t)$ is the input sensory frame at time $t$, and $\mathcal{F}_{\text{perceive}}$ is a learned perception function (e.g., a neural network). The output $s(t)$ is an **ethical state vector** indicating, for example, "pedestrian present, high harm potential."

**Explanation:** The robot translates its world model into ethical terms. This is the moment a robot "sees" a moral dimension.

### Step 3 — Evaluate Each Candidate Action

For each feasible action $a_i \in A$, compute an **ethical utility score** $U_{\text{eth}}(a_i)$.

$$U_{\text{eth}}(a_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{\vert E \vert} w_j \cdot V_j\!\left(a_i, s(t)\right)$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $w_j$ = weight (priority) of ethical principle $j$. Weights may be set by designers (top-down) or learned (bottom-up).
- $V_j(a_i, s(t))$ = degree of violation/satisfaction of principle $j$ under action $a_i$ given state $s(t)$.
- $\vert E \vert$ = cardinality of the ethical ontology (number of principles).

### Step 4 — Apply the Action-Selection Rule

Select the action that **maximises ethical utility** (or minimises harm, depending on the framework).

$$a^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{a_i \in A}\, U_{\text{eth}}\!\left(a_i\right)$$

**Explanation:** This is the moral decision — the action the robot will execute. For consequentialist frameworks, $a^{*}$ is the *least harmful* action. For deontological frameworks, $a^{*}$ is the action that *respects all inviolable rules* (and may be the only feasible action).

### Step 5 — Justify and Log

For auditability (a KTU-relevant requirement under ISO 13482 and IEC 61508):

$$\mathcal{L} \;=\; \bigl\{\, s(t),\, A,\, U_{\text{eth}}(a_1),\, \dots,\, U_{\text{eth}}(a_n),\, a^{*},\, \text{reason} \,\bigr\}$$

**Explanation:** Every ethical decision is logged in a tamper-evident record $\mathcal{L}$. This is the *audit trail* — necessary for legal accountability, post-incident analysis, and certification audits.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Autonomous Vehicle Collision Avoidance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Style Worked Example:**
> An autonomous vehicle faces an unavoidable accident. It must choose between three actions:
> - **$a_1$** = Swerve left → hits **1 pedestrian** on the sidewalk.
> - **$a_2$** = Swerve right → hits **1 cyclist** in the cycle lane.
> - **$a_3$** = Brake straight → collides with **2 passengers** inside the vehicle.

**Ethical Ontology:** $E = \{\text{harm}\}$ (simplified). Weight $w_{\text{harm}} = 1$.

**Violation Values** (number of individuals harmed, weighted by vulnerability: pedestrian 1.0, cyclist 1.2, passenger 1.0):

$$V_{\text{harm}}(a_1) = 1 \times 1.0 = 1.0$$

$$V_{\text{harm}}(a_2) = 1 \times 1.2 = 1.2$$

$$V_{\text{harm}}(a_3) = 2 \times 1.0 = 2.0$$

**Ethical Utility** (lower = better, so we minimise):

$$U_{\text{eth}}(a_1) = 1.0, \quad U_{\text{eth}}(a_2) = 1.2, \quad U_{\text{eth}}(a_3) = 2.0$$

**Action Selection:**

$$a^{*} = \arg\min_{a_i \in A}\, U_{\text{eth}}(a_i) = a_1 \quad \text{(swerve left)}$$

**Decision:** The vehicle swerves left.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Loss-of-Marks Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to assign **justification** for the chosen action — Marks are awarded for the *reasoning*, not just the answer.
> 2. Confusing **ethical utility maximisation** with **ethical utility minimisation** — the direction depends on the framework (consequentialism vs. deontology). Always state which framework you are using.
> 3. Not linking the decision back to **Moor's Level 2** — examiners want to see the theoretical framework applied, not just arithmetic.

## 3.4 Derivation — Why Pure Rule-Based Morality Fails

Consider Asimov's **Three Laws of Robotics** (formulated 1942):

1. A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
2. A robot must obey the orders given by humans, except where such orders conflict with Law 1.
3. A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this does not conflict with Laws 1 or 2.

**Limitation Proof (informal):**

Suppose a robot must choose between:
- **Path A:** Saves 1 human by inaction-allowing-harm of 5 humans (violates Law 1 by inaction).
- **Path B:** Actively harms 1 human to save 5 (violates the literal "may not injure" phrasing of Law 1).

Using a strict textual interpretation, both actions violate Law 1. The robot is paralysed — a contradiction called the **"inaction paradox"** in Asimov's novels. This proves:

$$ \text{Rigid Rule System} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Undecidability in Edge Cases} $$

**Resolution:** Move to **Moor Level 2** — explicit ethical reasoning with weighted principles, not rigid universal laws.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** Pure top-down rule systems (Wallach–Allen Top-Down extreme) are mathematically incapable of resolving all moral dilemmas. Therefore, real-world ethical robots must combine **top-down rules** with **bottom-up learning** — Wallach & Allen's middle-ground *Operative Morality* with selected *Autonomous Morality* features. This is the practical state of the art in 2025–2026.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Define Roboethics. Who coined the term and when?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Roboethics is a branch of applied ethics that examines the moral principles governing the design, construction, deployment, and use of robots, as well as the ethical consequences of human-robot interaction in society.
- **Origin (1 mark):** The term was formally coined by **Gianmarco Veruggio** during the **First International Symposium on Roboethics** held in **Sanremo, Italy, in January 2004**, under the auspices of the IEEE Robotics and Automation Society.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Differentiate between Roboethics and Robomorality with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Roboethics | Robomorality |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Scope** | Ethics *about* robots (human responsibility) | Ethics *by* robots (machine capability) |
| **Stakeholder** | Humans — designers, users, regulators | The robot / autonomous system itself |
| **Example** | A company following ISO 10218 while installing a cobot | A cobot that auto-stops upon detecting unexpected human contact |

- (1 mark) Correct definition of Roboethics with example.
- (1 mark) Correct definition of Robomorality with example.
- (1 mark) Clear differentiation highlighted.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail **James H. Moor's four levels of ethical agency** in machines, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Veruggio's three operational levels of Roboethics**. How are they linked to current ISO standards for industrial and collaborative robots? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

- **(2 marks)** Introduce James Moor (Dartmouth philosopher) and his 2006 framework *"The Nature, Importance, and Difficulty of Machine Ethics."*
- **(1 mark)** **Level 0 — Ethical Impact Agents:** No ethical sensitivity; the system affects ethics without knowing. *Example:* Industrial welding arm — no awareness of downstream worker harm. Moral responsibility rests entirely with humans.
- **(1 mark)** **Level 1 — Implicit Ethical Agents:** Built-in ethical constraints prevent unethical outcomes. *Example:* Surgical robot (Da Vinci) with software-defined no-go anatomical zones.
- **(1 mark)** **Level 2 — Explicit Ethical Agents:** System identifies ethical features and reasons using formal methods (deontic logic, utility calculus). *Example:* Autonomous vehicle selecting minimum-harm collision path.
- **(1 mark)** **Level 3 — Full Ethical Agents:** Machine has consciousness, intentionality, free will; bears direct moral responsibility. Currently speculative — no machine qualifies.
- **(1 mark)** Concluding summary: Moor's framework is **descriptive**, it *describes* moral capability, it does not prescribe solutions. Levels 0 and 1 are engineering problems; Levels 2 and 3 are philosophical / AI-research problems.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

- **(1 mark)** Introduce Veruggio's 2004 framework as **operationally oriented** — designed for real-world deployment.
- **(1.5 marks)** **L1 — Operational Ethics:** Ethics applied by humans during design and use. Robot has zero moral awareness. Example: factory safety cage designed to ISO 10218-1.
- **(1.5 marks)** **L2 — Programmed Ethics:** Ethics embedded in robot via software/hardware limits. Example: cobot (e.g., Universal Robots UR5e) with power-and-force limiting per ISO/TS 15066.
- **(1.5 marks)** **L3 — Autonomous Moral Reasoning:** Robot evaluates dilemmas and decides independently. Example: autonomous vehicle in no-win collision scenarios (under research per ISO/DIS 39001).
- **(1.5 marks)** **Linkage to ISO Standards:**
  - **L1 → ISO 10218-1 & 10218-2** (Industrial robot safety requirements).
  - **L1/L2 → ISO/TS 15066** (Collaborative robot guidance, including safety-rated monitored stop, hand guiding, speed & separation monitoring, power & force limiting).
  - **L2 → IEC 61508 / IEC 62061** (Functional safety of safety-related control systems).
  - **L3 → ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** and emerging **ISO 39001** for autonomous vehicle road-traffic safety.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model]

**(a)** Explain **Wallach and Allen's framework of Machine Morality**. Discuss the two dimensions — *top-down* and *bottom-up* moral development — with one engineering example of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable mathematical formulation, show how an **Explicit Ethical Agent (Moor Level 2)** evaluates and selects an action in a moral dilemma. Illustrate with a numerical example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

- **(1 mark)** Authors: Wendell Wallach (Yale Interdisciplinary Center for Bioethics) and Colin Allen (Indiana University), in their 2009 book *Moral Machines: Teaching Robots Right from Wrong*.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Two Dimensions:**
  - **Top-Down (Operative Morality):** Rules, principles, and constraints programmed by designers. Example: Asimov's Three Laws encoded into a service robot; or the obstacle-avoidance rule "do not enter pedestrian zones" in a delivery robot.
  - **Bottom-Up (Autonomous Morality):** Moral behaviour learned from data or human demonstrations. Example: An autonomous vehicle trained via *inverse reinforcement learning* on human driving data, learning implicit ethical preferences.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Three Classes of Systems:**
  1. **Ethical Impact Systems** (no awareness) — same as Moor Level 0.
  2. **Ethically Operative Systems** (built-in rules) — hybrid of Moor L1 + L2.
  3. **Ethically Autonomous Systems** (learned + reasoned) — approaches Moor L2/L3.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Why Both Dimensions Are Needed:** Pure top-down fails in novel situations (Asimov's inaction paradox); pure bottom-up is unpredictable and unsafe for deployment. The **middle-ground hybrid** is the practical state of the art.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Engineering Example of Hybrid:** Modern autonomous vehicles use *top-down* safety rules (e.g., "stop at red lights") and *bottom-up* learned driving policies (lane-keeping, gap acceptance). This is **Wallach–Allen Ethically Operative** in action.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(Complete derivation from Section 3.2 reproduced as a structured exam answer.)**

- **(1 mark)** **Step 1 — Ethical Ontology $E$:** Define $E = \{\text{harm}, \text{fairness}, \text{consent}, \text{truth}, \text{autonomy}, \text{privacy}\}$ as the set of moral categories the agent recognises.
- **(1 mark)** **Step 2 — Ethical State Sensing:** $s(t) = \mathcal{F}_{\text{perceive}}(I(t))$ — map raw sensors $I(t)$ into an ethical state vector $s(t)$.
- **(1 mark)** **Step 3 — Ethical Utility Evaluation:**
$$U_{\text{eth}}(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{\vert E \vert} w_j \cdot V_j(a_i, s(t))$$
Explain each term (weights $w_j$, violations $V_j$, cardinality $\vert E \vert$).
- **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Action Selection:** $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a_i \in A} U_{\text{eth}}(a_i)$ — the action that maximises ethical utility.
- **(2 marks)** **Numerical Example** (use the autonomous-vehicle worked example from Section 3.3): Present three candidate actions, calculate violation values for each, apply $\arg\min$ (or $\arg\max$ with sign convention), and conclude $a^{*} = a_1$ (swerve left). State clearly that this is a **Moor Level 2** reasoning step, not Level 1.
- **(1 mark)** **Justification & Logging:** Conclude by mentioning the audit log $\mathcal{L}$ stored for post-incident review and certification compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the frameworks** — Students often write "Moor's Veruggio's levels" or mix Wallach–Allen with Moor. Always **name the author** when introducing a framework. *(-2 marks if conflated.)*
> 2. **Listing without examples** — Each level MUST have at least one concrete engineering example. Generic "robots in factories" is not enough. *(-1 mark per level without example.)*
> 3. **Forgetting ISO linkage** — Part (b) of Question A explicitly asks for ISO standard linkage. Omitting ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066, or IEC 61508 costs 2 marks.
> 4. **In the numerical question** — Many students skip the *justification* for $a^{*}$. The marks for "reasoning" are awarded to the explanation, not the arithmetic.
> 5. **Writing "Roboethics = Robomorality"** — This is a factual error. They are related but distinct concepts. *(-1 mark minimum.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- **Roboethics** (Veruggio, 2004) = Ethics *about* robots, a **human responsibility**.
- **Robomorality** = Ethics *by* robots, a **machine capability**.
- **James Moor (2006)** proposed **4 levels**: Ethical Impact → Implicit → Explicit → Full Ethical Agent.
- **Wallach & Allen (2009)** proposed **3 classes** + 2D spectrum (Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up).
- **Veruggio (2004)** proposed **3 operational levels**: L1 Operational, L2 Programmed, L3 Autonomous Reasoning.
- **Moor Level 0** → no awareness, but ethical impact exists.
- **Moor Level 1** → embedded rules (e.g., cobot safety stops).
- **Moor Level 2** → ethical reasoning (autonomous vehicles, surgical decision support).
- **Moor Level 3** → full moral agency — **theoretical**, no real-world implementation.
- **Asimov's Three Laws** are a **top-down** morality framework and suffer from the **inaction paradox**.
- **Real-world ethical agents** are **hybrid** — combining top-down rules + bottom-up learning.
- **ISO 10218-1/2** governs industrial robots (links to Veruggio L1).
- **ISO/TS 15066** governs collaborative robots (links to Veruggio L2).
- **IEC 61508 / IEC 62061** govern functional safety of robot control systems.
- **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** addresses safety of intended functionality for autonomous systems (links to Veruggio L3).
- **Audit logging** $\mathcal{L} = \{ s(t), A, U_{\text{eth}}(a_1), \dots, a^{*}, \text{reason} \}$ is mandatory for certification and post-incident analysis.
- The **ethical utility formula** $U_{\text{eth}}(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{\vert E \vert} w_j \cdot V_j(a_i, s(t))$ and the **decision rule** $a^{*} = \arg\max U_{\text{eth}}(a_i)$ must be memorised verbatim for 14-mark numerical questions.
- Always state the **framework author and year** when answering — never mix frameworks without attribution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethics Fundamental Elements and Theories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT417 - ROBOT SAFETY AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ethics Fundamental Elements and Theories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Safety and Standards (PERAT417)**, *Ethics* refers to the systematic, rational, and disciplined study of **moral principles**, **values**, **duties**, and **virtues** that govern the design, deployment, operation, and deactivation of robotic and autonomous systems. It provides the normative philosophical framework that guides engineers, designers, and policymakers in making decisions when robotic systems interact with **human agents**, **societal structures**, and the **environment**.

The three principal branches of ethics relevant to robotics are:

1. **Meta-Ethics** — The study of the *nature* of ethical judgments themselves (e.g., Are moral truths objective? What does "good" mean?).
2. **Normative Ethics** — The study of *which* actions are right or wrong, and *why* (e.g., Utilitarianism, Deontology, Virtue Ethics).
3. **Applied Ethics** — The practical application of ethical theory to real-world domains such as **robot ethics**, **AI ethics**, **bioethics**, and **engineering ethics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PERAT417 Module 3, students are expected to (i) identify the *fundamental elements* of ethics, (ii) compare the major *normative theories*, and (iii) apply these to robotic system lifecycles. Pure memorization of theory names is **insufficient** — examiners reward *applied reasoning* and *case-based argumentation*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of ethics as the **"operating system" of human decision-making**, while robotic engineering is the **"hardware specification"** of what a robot *can* do.

- A robot can physically apply a 500 N force (hardware capability).
- Should it apply that force to a child who just ran into its path? (ethical question).
- **Ethics is the moral "firmware"** that translates a machine's capability into *justifiable* action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Three Lenses":**
> Imagine you are a civil engineer building a bridge. You check three lenses before opening it to the public:
> 1. **Meta-Ethical Lens** — "What does 'public safety' truly *mean*?"
> 2. **Normative Lens** — "Which engineering standard *maximizes* safety vs. cost?"
> 3. **Applied Lens** — "In *this* specific river condition, which material do I use?"
> Robotics ethics works the same way: philosophers debate (meta), theorists prescribe (normative), engineers apply (applied).

### 1.3 Fundamental Elements of Ethics

Ethics is built upon **seven irreducible fundamental elements**. Each is a building block without which ethical reasoning collapses.

| # | Element | Definition | Robotics Example |
|---|---------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Moral Agent** | An entity capable of *deliberate action* and *moral accountability* | A human programmer writing path-planning logic |
| 2 | **Moral Patient** | An entity whose welfare *matters morally* but who may not be a moral agent | A pedestrian, an animal, an autonomous car itself |
| 3 | **Values** | Enduring beliefs about what is *worth* pursuing (e.g., safety, autonomy) | "Human life is inviolable" |
| 4 | **Principles** | Action-guiding rules derived from values (e.g., "Do no harm") | Asimov's First Law |
| 5 | **Duties / Obligations** | Things one *must* do regardless of consequence | A robot must report a fault to its operator |
| 6 | **Virtues** | Stable character traits enabling ethical action | Honesty, prudence, justice in AI designers |
| 7 | **Consequences / Outcomes** | The actual results of an action, judged for moral weight | A swarm robot's mission outcome |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction to remember:** A *Moral Agent* acts; a *Moral Patient* is acted upon. A robot, in the strict philosophical sense, is **not yet a full moral agent** (it lacks genuine understanding), but it is increasingly treated as a *moral patient* in frameworks like the **IEEE 7000-series standards** and the **EU AI Liability Directive (2022/0303)**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Robotic Ethics

While ethics is qualitative, several **measurable proxy metrics** are standardized:

- **IEC 61508 SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** SIL 1 → SIL 4, where SIL 4 corresponds to a *probability of dangerous failure per hour* of **< 10⁻⁸ to < 10⁻⁹**.
- **ISO 13849-1 Performance Level (PL):** PL a → PL e.
- **IEEE 7000-series** (e.g., IEEE 7001 for transparency, IEEE 7002 for data privacy).
- **Asimov Compliance Score** — a *qualitative* engineering metric evaluating how closely a robot's behavior adheres to codified ethical rulesets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Normative Ethics — Comparative Analysis

Normative ethics provides the **prescriptive rules** of right and wrong. KTU examiners expect a clear comparative analysis of the **three major schools**, plus a working knowledge of two contemporary extensions.

#### 2.1.1 Utilitarianism (Consequentialism)

- **Founder (modern form):** Jeremy Bentham (1748–1832) and John Stuart Mill (1806–1873).
- **Core Claim:** An action is morally right if and only if it *maximizes overall utility* (typically interpreted as happiness, welfare, or preference satisfaction).
- **Decision Rule:** Choose the action that produces the **greatest good for the greatest number**.
- **Two Sub-types:**
  - **Act Utilitarianism** — Evaluate each act in isolation.
  - **Rule Utilitarianism** — Follow rules that, when generally adopted, maximize utility.
- **Robotics Application:** In autonomous-vehicle crash algorithms, utilitarianism justifies algorithms that minimize *total* expected casualties, even if it means sacrificing the passenger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bentham's Hedonic Calculus** quantifies utility using 7 variables: *intensity, duration, certainty, propinquity, fecundity, purity, extent*. In modern robotics, this is mirrored by **expected-utility decision theory** used in reinforcement learning reward functions.

#### 2.1.2 Deontological Ethics (Kantian Ethics)

- **Founder:** Immanuel Kant (1724–1804).
- **Core Claim:** An action is morally right based on its **adherence to duty and universal moral law**, *not* on its consequences.
- **Central Concept — The Categorical Imperative (CI):**
  1. **Formula of Universal Law:** "Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law."
  2. **Formula of Humanity:** "Act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, always at the same time as an end and never merely as a means."
  3. **Formula of the Kingdom of Ends:** Act as if your maxims were to be made universal law by all rational agents.
- **Robotics Application:** A deontological medical robot may *refuse* to lie to a patient even if deception would reduce short-term suffering, because honesty is a categorical duty.

#### 2.1.3 Virtue Ethics

- **Founders:** Aristotle (384–322 BCE); modern revival by Alasdair MacIntyre, Martha Nussbaum.
- **Core Claim:** Ethics is about *becoming* a good person, not just *doing* good actions. Emphasizes **character, virtues, and flourishing (eudaimonia)**.
- **Key Virtues Relevant to Robotics Designers:** *Prudence, Justice, Honesty, Courage, Temperance, Responsibility, Accountability.*
- **Robotics Application:** A virtuous AI design team *proactively* anticipates misuse (e.g., banning real-time facial recognition for social scoring) because prudence and justice are part of their professional character.

#### 2.1.4 Contemporary Extensions

| Theory | Core Idea | Robotics Application |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Care Ethics** (Carol Gilligan, Nel Noddings) | Ethics rooted in *relationships, empathy, and contextual response* | Eldercare robots should preserve dignity, not just complete tasks |
| **Rights-Based Ethics** (John Locke, Tom Beauchamp) | Moral agents hold *inviolable rights*; acts violating rights are wrong | Robots must not violate human autonomy, privacy, or self-determination |

### 2.2 Meta-Ethical Positions (Brief but Examinable)

KTU may ask a 3-mark question testing *meta-ethical* distinctions. Memorize the contrast between these three:

- **Moral Realism** — Moral truths are *objective*, mind-independent (like physical facts).
- **Moral Anti-Realism** — Moral truths are *subjective* or *mind-dependent* (e.g., emotivism, prescriptivism).
- **Moral Relativism** — Moral truth varies *by culture or context* (a *meta-ethical* claim, distinct from normative relativism).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **last-day revision sheet**. KTU examiners love asking students to *classify* a scenario under the correct theory. Master the **decision criterion** column.

| Theory | Decision Criterion | Key Word | Key Proponent | Robot Ethics Tendency |
|--------|-------------------|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Utilitarianism | $\max \sum U_i$ | *Outcomes* | Bentham, Mill | Sacrifices minority for majority |
| Deontology | Universalizability test | *Duty* | Kant | Inviolable rules; rights respected |
| Virtue Ethics | Character alignment with eudaimonia | *Character* | Aristotle | Designer virtue → product virtue |
| Care Ethics | Maintains/strengthens relationships | *Empathy* | Gilligan | Dignity-preserving interactions |
| Rights-Based | No right is violated | *Rights* | Locke | Hard constraints on action |
| Relativism | Cultural consensus | *Context* | Westermarck | Standards vary by society |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** When writing absolute value or norm in your own notes, use $\lvert x \rvert$ (LaTeX escaped bars) **not** plain `|x|` if you're authoring markdown that might be parsed as a table delimiter.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These theories are not *philosophical decoration*. They directly drive:

- **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design (EAD) standards** — heavily deontological (rights-based).
- **EU AI Act (2024)** — combines consequentialist risk-tiering with deontological hard prohibitions (e.g., Article 5 bans social scoring).
- **Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics** — a deontological rule-set.
- **Reward shaping in RL agents** — utilitarian at the mathematical level.
- **Design-for-Dignity frameworks** (e.g., Microsoft's "Guidance for Responsible AI") — virtue + care ethics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Ethics is qualitative, so the "derivations" here are **conceptual decision algorithms** — the kind examiners award full marks to when explicitly laid out. The section also derives the *expected-utility function* used in utilitarian autonomous-system decision making.

### 3.1 Derivation: The Utilitarian Decision Algorithm (Expected-Utility Form)

This is the most common quantitative framework examiners expect for **utilitarian-class** robotics questions (e.g., autonomous vehicle ethics, search-and-rescue robot allocation).

**Step 1 — Identify the set of possible actions.**

Let $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$ be the set of all permissible actions available to the robot at decision time $t$.

**Step 2 — Identify the set of affected moral patients.**

Let $P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_m\}$ be the set of moral patients (humans, animals, property, environment) impacted by the action.

**Step 3 — Assign a utility value to each patient–outcome pair.**

For each action $a_i$ and each patient $p_j$, define the *utility* $U(a_i, p_j) \in \mathbb{R}$. Conventionally, *positive* utility indicates benefit, *negative* indicates harm.

**Step 4 — Compute the total expected utility for each action.**

$$
E[U(a_i)] = \sum_{j=1}^{m} U(a_i, p_j) \cdot P(a_i \mid p_j)
$$

where $P(a_i \mid p_j)$ is the conditional probability that action $a_i$ leads to a state affecting $p_j$.

**Step 5 — Select the action that maximizes expected utility.**

$$
a^{*} = \arg\max_{a_i \in A} \; E[U(a_i)]
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the *act vs. rule* distinction (exam-relevant).**

- *Act utilitarian:* choose $a^{*}$ in this specific instance.
- *Rule utilitarian:* ask: "If a *rule* 'always choose $a$-type actions' were universalized, would expected utility rise?" If yes → adopt the rule; if no → reject the rule even for this instance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Textual Justification of each step:** Step 1 ensures *scope* of analysis is bounded. Step 2 is the *moral patient* enumeration. Step 3 implements Bentham's hedonic calculus quantitatively. Step 4 aggregates via linearity (Mill's qualitative-vs-quantitative utility). Step 5 is the **maximization principle**. Step 6 is the bridge to rule-utilitarianism and deontological safety rules.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Deontological Universalizability Test

This is the procedure Kant prescribes for testing a *maxim* (a personal principle of action) for moral permissibility.

**Step 1 — State the proposed maxim explicitly.**

A maxim has the form: "In situation $S$, I will perform action $A$ in order to achieve end $E$."

**Step 2 — Universalize the maxim.**

Replace the first-person singular with all rational agents: "In situation $S$, *every* agent performs $A$ to achieve $E$."

**Step 3 — Test for self-consistency.**

Ask: "Could the world, in which this maxim is universally followed, *itself* exist or function coherently?"

**Step 4 — Test for instrumental consistency.**

Ask: "Could the agent who universalizes the maxim *still* achieve their original end $E$?"

**Step 5 — Decision rule.**

$$
\text{Maxim is permissible} \iff (\text{Steps 3 AND 4 both pass})
$$

If either step fails, the maxim is morally impermissible — *regardless of consequences*.

**Step 6 — Apply the Formula of Humanity.**

Independently verify: "Does the action treat any person (or moral patient) as *merely* a means to an end?" If yes → impermissible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering application:** The Universalizability test is the philosophical backbone of the **"do not release unsafe code"** maxim in software engineering. Universalize "ship safety-critical code without testing" → catastrophe. Hence it fails Step 3 and is deontologically forbidden.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Applying Theories to a Robotic Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Scenario (used in KTU sample questions):** An autonomous warehouse robot detects a fallen human worker in its path. Continuing its task saves the company \$10,000 in downtime; stopping and assisting the worker saves the worker's life (probability 0.9) but causes \$10,000 loss. What *should* the robot do?

| Theory | Analysis | Recommended Action | Marks-Bait Phrase |
|--------|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Utilitarianism (Act)** | Total utility = saved life (high positive) − \$10,000 loss (low negative) | **Stop and assist** | "Maximizes aggregate welfare" |
| **Deontology** | The maxim "robots may harm humans to save money" fails universalizability | **Stop and assist** | "Categorical duty not to instrumentalize humans" |
| **Virtue Ethics** | A virtuous robot *exhibits* compassion and responsibility | **Stop and assist** | "Acts in accordance with eudaimonia for the community" |
| **Care Ethics** | The relationship worker↔employer is fiduciary; care dominates cost | **Stop and assist** | "Maintains the caring relationship" |
| **Rights-Based** | The worker's right to life is inviolable | **Stop and assist** | "No right may be traded for money" |
| **Moral Relativism** | Outcome depends on the local jurisdiction's norms | **Indeterminate** | "No universal answer; depends on society" |
| **Utilitarianism (Rule, with caveat)** | Universal rule "robots always stop for fallen humans" → long-term trust → greater utility | **Stop and assist** | "Rule yields greater expected utility than ad-hoc trade-off" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark Loss:** Students often *only* mention the theory's name without applying the *decision criterion*. To secure full marks, **explicitly state the criterion first**, then apply it to the facts.

### 3.4 Mapping Ethics Elements to Robot Lifecycle Phases

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a *high-yield visual* that KTU examiners frequently test.

| Lifecycle Phase | Relevant Ethical Element | KTU-Standard Concern |
|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Design** | Virtues of designer | Bias embedded in training data |
| **Development** | Duty of due diligence | Code review & testing rigor |
| **Testing** | Non-maleficence | Safety under edge cases |
| **Deployment** | Justice, fairness | Equitable access & distribution |
| **Operation** | Rights, autonomy | Informed consent, transparency |
| **Maintenance** | Accountability | Audit trails, explainability |
| **Deactivation / Recycling** | Responsibility, dignity | Right-to-repair, e-waste ethics |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Three Branches of Ethics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ethics as a Discipline] --> B[Meta-Ethics]
    A --> C[Normative Ethics]
    A --> D[Applied Ethics]
    B --> B1[What is the nature of moral truth]
    B --> B2[Moral Realism vs Anti-Realism]
    B --> B3[Sources of moral authority]
    C --> C1[Utilitarianism - Consequentialist]
    C --> C2[Deontological Ethics - Duty Based]
    C --> C3[Virtue Ethics - Character Based]
    C --> C4[Care Ethics - Relationship Based]
    C --> C5[Rights Based Ethics - Liberty Centric]
    D --> D1[Robot Ethics]
    D --> D2[AI Ethics]
    D --> D3[Engineering Ethics]
    D --> D4[Bioethics and Environmental Ethics]
    C1 --> R1[Applied to Robot Decision Algorithms]
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R2[Applied to Designer Culture and Governance]
    C4 --> R3[Applied to Human Robot Interaction]
    C5 --> R4[Applied to Data Privacy and Autonomy]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow of the Utilitarian Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Decision State] --> S2[Enumerate Available Actions A]
    S2 --> S3[Enumerate Moral Patients P]
    S3 --> S4[Assign Utility Matrix U for each Action Patient pair]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Expected Utility for each Action]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Rule Utilitarian Filter]
    S6 --> S7{Is action consistent with universalized rule}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Select Maximum Expected Utility Action]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Reject Action and Recompute]
    S9 --> S5
    S8 --> S10[Execute Action and Log for Accountability]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Deontological Universalizability Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Proposed Maxim] --> K2[Universalize the Maxim]
    K2 --> K3{Could the universalized world exist coherently}
    K3 -- No --> K4[Impermissible - Reject Action]
    K3 -- Yes --> K5{Could the agent still achieve the original end}
    K5 -- No --> K6[Impermissible - Self Defeating]
    K5 -- Yes --> K7{Does the action treat any person as mere means}
    K7 -- Yes --> K8[Impermissible - Violates Formula of Humanity]
    K7 -- No --> K9[Permissible - Categorically Allowed]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Mapping Fundamental Elements to Robot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Moral Agent] --> EA[Human Designer Operator]
    E2[Moral Patient] --> EB[Human End User Environment Robot Itself]

    E4[Principles] --> ED[Do No Harm Informed Consent Transparency]
    E5[Duties] --> EE[Fault Reporting Safety Lock Accountability]
    E6[Virtues] --> EF[Prudence Justice Honesty Courage]
    E7[Consequences] --> EG[Expected Utility Harm Distribution Long Term Impact]
    EA --> RS[Robotic System]
    EB --> RS
    EC --> RS
    ED --> RS
    EE --> RS
    EF --> RS
    EG --> RS
    RS --> ST[Compliant with IEEE 7000 EU AI Act ISO 13482 ISO 10218]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Comparative Theory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U[Utilitarianism] --- U1[Consequence Maximizing]
    U --- U2[Quantitative Hedonic Calculus]
    D[Deontology] --- D1[Duty Based]
    D --- D2[Categorical Imperative]
    V[Virtue Ethics] --- V1[Character Based]
    V --- V2[Eudaimonia Flourishing]
    C[Care Ethics] --- C1[Relationship Based]
    C --- C2[Empathic Response]

    RL --- RL2[Culturally Variable]
    U -. Contrasts .-> D
    D -. Contrasts .-> U
    V -. Complements .-> D
    C -. Complements .-> V
    R -. Constrains .-> U
    RL -. Denies universality to .-> D
```
