## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Differentiate between Stop Category 0, Stop Category 1, and Stop Category 2 as defined in ISO 10218-1. State one industrial application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Stop Category | Mechanism | Drive Power State | Braking | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Immediate uncontrolled | **Removed immediately** | Mechanical (uncontrolled) | Weld tip near operator, hazardous energy removal |
| **1** | Controlled deceleration | **Maintained until stop**, then removed | Controlled dynamic + mechanical | Service mode, teach pendant, hazardous setup |
| **2** | Monitored stop | **Maintained** | Drive actively holds position | Production, safeguarded cell, no removal of torque |

> Stop Category 0 and 1 are "protective stops" that **interrupt hazardous motion**; Category 2 is a "operational stop" used when the safeguard permits continued power.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List and briefly explain the four collaborative operation modes specified in ISO/TS 15066.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** Robot halts and remains stationary while operator is inside the collaborative workspace; no separation required when stopped.
2. **Hand Guiding (HG):** Operator physically moves the robot via an enabling device and/or force-sensing handle at a **maximum speed of 250 mm/s**.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** Robot speed is dynamically reduced as the operator's separation distance decreases, using safety-rated vision or scanners.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** The cobot's mechanical design (compliant joints, current/torque monitoring) clamps contact forces and pressures below the biomechanical thresholds in **Annex A** of ISO/TS 15066.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
**a)** Describe in detail the **three-pillar safety architecture** (inherent, engineered, administrative) for industrial robot systems as codified in ISO 12100. Illustrate with one practical example for each pillar. (7 marks)

**b)** A perimeter light curtain in a 6-axis robot cell has response time **t₁ = 20 ms**, robot worst-case stop time **t₂ = 520 ms**, and is mounted at floor level. Compute the **minimum installation distance S** per ISO 13855. Use K = 1,600 mm/s and C = 850 mm. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **three-pillar (3-step) risk reduction methodology** is the canonical framework specified in **ISO 12100:2010** §6.2 and is mirrored in **ANSI B11.0** and **ISO 10218-2** for robot cells. The pillars are applied **sequentially in descending order of reliability**; protective measures from a more effective pillar are not bypassed by adding measures from a less effective one.

**Pillar 1 — Inherent Safe Design Measures (Most Effective):**
These eliminate or reduce the hazard at the source during the design phase itself, so that no human–machine interaction with the dangerous element is required.
- *Robot cell example:* Reducing the robot's maximum TCP speed from 3 m/s to 0.5 m/s through a software-reduced speed override at the safety controller. This reduces kinetic energy from 225 J (at 50 kg payload) to 6.25 J — below the ISO/TS 15066 biomechanical injury threshold.

**Pillar 2 — Engineered Safeguarding (Intermediate):**
If the hazard cannot be fully eliminated, **physical or electronic protective devices** are interposed between the hazard and the operator.
- *Robot cell example:* A **Type 4 safety light curtain** (per IEC 61496) installed at the cell perimeter, hardwired to a **safety relay** (per ISO 13849-1 PL d) that triggers a Stop Category 0 when the operator's torso breaks the optical plane.

**Pillar 3 — Administrative Controls (Least Reliable):**
Procedural and behavioral measures that depend on **human compliance**.
- *Robot cell example:* A **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) program** per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147, mandating that every maintenance intervention begins with a documented energy-isolation procedure and a personal padlock on the main disconnect.

> The hierarchy matters because administrative controls (signs, training) are bypassed in ~60% of industrial accidents, whereas engineered safeguards fail in <1% of cases per OSHA statistics.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** t₁ = 20 ms = 0.020 s; t₂ = 520 ms = 0.520 s; K = 1,600 mm/s; C = 850 mm (floor-level light curtain, per ISO 13855 Table 1).

**Step 1 — Total stopping time:**

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = 0.020 + 0.520 = 0.540 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the safety distance equation:**

$$S = (K \times T) + C = (1{,}600 \times 0.540) + 850$$

$$S = 864 + 850 = 1{,}714 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Round up to next 10 mm increment per ISO 13855 convention:**

$$\boxed{S_{min} = 1{,}720 \text{ mm} \approx 1.72 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using stop distance:** Robot travel during stop = v_max × T + ½ a T². Assuming v_max = 1,800 mm/s and a = -2,500 mm/s², robot travels 486 mm — well within the 1,720 mm buffer.

---

### 5.3 Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
**a)** Explain the **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** collaborative robot safety concept per ISO/TS 15066. Describe the biomechanical model used and list the **maximum permissible transient and quasi-static contact forces** for at least **five body regions**. (7 marks)

**b)** During maintenance, a robot drive cabinet has **C = 4,700 µF**, **R = 100 kΩ** (active discharge), and DC bus voltage **V₀ = 750 V**. The safety touch threshold is **V_safe = 50 V**. Compute the **minimum LOTO wait time**. If the manufacturer's printed wait time is 4 minutes (240 s), determine the **percentage safety margin**. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** is one of the four collaborative robot operation modes defined in **ISO/TS 15066:2016** (the others being SRMS, HG, and SSM). PFL relies on **inherent mechanical and control design** to clamp the **transient and quasi-static contact energy** that can be transferred to a human body region during an unintended collision below the level that causes **pain, injury, or impairment**.

**Biomechanical Model — Spring–Damper Analogy:**
ISO/TS 15066 Annex A models each of the 29 human body regions as a **virtual mass-spring-damper system**. The contact force is the sum of an elastic (quasi-static) and a viscous (transient) term:

$$F_{contact}(x, \dot{x}) = k \cdot x_{compression} + c \cdot \dot{x}_{compression}$$

The standard tabulates **maximum permissible quasi-static contact pressure (N/cm²)** and **maximum transient contact force (N)** for each region, derived from cadaver and volunteer impact testing. **Five representative values:**

| Body Region | Max Quasi-Static Pressure | Max Transient Force |
|---|---|---|
| Skull / Forehead | **130 N** | **175 N** |
| Chest | **140 N** | **250 N** |
| Abdomen | **110 N** | **185 N** |
| Hand / Fingers | **140 N** | **275 N** |
| Thigh | **220 N** | **315 N** |

**Engineering Implementation:** Cobots such as **Universal Robots UR5e** and **KUKA LBR iiwa** achieve PFL compliance by:
- **Compliant joint torque sensors** detecting external force in < 1 ms.
- **Series elastic actuators (SEA)** absorbing transient impact energy.
- **Software current limits** clamping motor torque below the force budget.
- **Virtual safety zones** mapped in the safety controller, dynamically reducing speed as the operator approaches.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the RC time constant:**

$$\tau = R \times C = 100{,}000 \times 4{,}700 \times 10^{-6} = 47 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the safe discharge time using the LOTO formula:**

$$t_{safe} = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_0}{V_{safe}}\right) = 47 \times \ln\left(\frac{750}{50}\right)$$

$$t_{safe} = 47 \times \ln(15) = 47 \times 2.708 = 127.28 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Manufacturer's wait time:**

$$t_{mfr} = 240 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Safety margin computation:**

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{t_{mfr} - t_{safe}}{t_{safe}} \times 100\% = \frac{240 - 127.28}{127.28} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{112.72}{127.28} \times 100\% = 88.56\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Safety margin} \approx 88.6\% \implies \text{Manufacturer's 240-s wait is COMPLIANT}}$$

> The 4-minute wait also covers **other energy sources** (24 V SMPS hold-up, hydraulic accumulators, pneumatic reservoirs), which is why the margin is intentionally large.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "C" constant** in the safety distance equation — drops 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up transient vs. quasi-static force limits** — the cobot force budget is NOT the same as the steady-state clamping force.
> 3. **Converting ms to s incorrectly** — always show units.
> 4. **Citing "R = S × P"** (omitting A) — the avoidance factor is the **distinguishing** feature of robot risk assessment.
> 5. **Confusing PL and SIL** — they are NOT the same scale; PL is for ISO 13849-1 hardware reliability, SIL is for IEC 62061 systematic capability.
> 6. **Skipping the LOTO discharge derivation** — state the formula and the units explicitly.
> 7. **Failing to mention the three pillars in order** — the descending order (inherent → engineered → administrative) carries 2 marks by itself.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Industrial Robot Safety Requirements:**
> - **Risk Score:** R = S × P × A. **R ≥ 12 → UNACCEPTABLE** (must re-engineer); 4–11 → ALARP; ≤ 3 → Acceptable.
> - **Three Pillars (ISO 12100):** Inherent (most effective) → Engineered → Administrative (least reliable). **Always apply in this order.**
> - **Key Standards:** **ISO 10218-1/-2** (industrial robot safety), **ISO/TS 15066** (cobots), **ISO 13849-1** (PL a–e), **IEC 62061** (SIL 1–3 for robots), **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** (LOTO), **ISO 13855** (safety distance), **ISO 12100** (general risk assessment).
> - **Safety Distance Equation:** S = (K × T) + C, with K = **1,600 mm/s** (hand-arm) or **2,000 mm/s** (whole body); C = 850 mm for floor-level light curtain.
> - **Stop Categories:** 0 (hard, uncontrolled, immediate power removal), 1 (controlled stop then power off), 2 (monitored stop, power maintained).
> - **Stop Categories & PL:** E-stop must be at least **PL d / SIL 2**; collaborative PFL must be **PL e / SIL 3**.
> - **Cobot 4 Modes:** SRMS, Hand-Guiding (≤ 250 mm/s), SSM, PFL.
> - **PFL Force Limits (ISO/TS 15066):** Skull 130 N, Chest 140 N (quasi-static); transient forces can be 1.5–2× higher.
> - **PFH_D values for PL:** PL a = 10⁻⁵, PL b = 10⁻⁶, PL c = 10⁻⁶, PL d = 10⁻⁷, PL e = 10⁻⁸ (per hour).
> - **LOTO DC-bus discharge:** t_safe = RC × ln(V₀ / V_safe); OSHA threshold = **50 V**; typical wait 3–5 min covers all auxiliary supplies.
> - **Biomechanical model:** F = kx + cẋ (Hookean + viscous damping).
> - **Energy Hierarchy:** Inherent > Safeguarding > Indicating devices > Training > PPE.
> - **Maintenance Hazards:** Stored electrical (DC bus, capacitors), pneumatic (accumulators, 6–10 bar), hydraulic (210 bar), gravitational (counterweight springs), and thermal (motor housings, weld tips).
> - **Sensory Safeguards:** Type 4 light curtains (IEC 61496), safety mats (ISO 13856-1), laser scanners (ISO 13849-1 PL d).
> - **Human-Robot Distance:** SSM separation is computed from robot stopping distance + operator approach speed × time-to-stop.
> - **Six-axis robot stopping time:** Typical 250–600 ms; **must be measured** for the actual worst-case payload, speed, and temperature.
> - **Workspace restriction:** Use **Safety-Rated Soft Axis and Space Limiting (SRASL)** to bound robot motion without physical hard stops.
> - **The acronym SLAM** (Safety Lifecycle Analysis Method) is sometimes used; the canonical method is the **V-Model of IEC 61508**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Design for Industrial Robot Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT417 - ROBOT SAFETY AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Design for Industrial Robot Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Design for Industrial Robot Systems** is the disciplined, standards-based engineering process of integrating **inherent safe design measures, safeguarding devices, complementary protective devices, and information for use** into a robotic workcell so as to eliminate or adequately reduce hazards arising from the robot's automatic, high-energy, multi-axis motion, payload, end-effector, and stored energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic).

According to **ISO 10218-1:2011** (Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 1: Robots) and **ISO 10218-2:2011** (Part 2: Robot systems and integration), safety design is structured as a **risk-reduction loop** (often called the *"3-step method"*):

1. **Inherent safe design measures** (built-in, by the manufacturer)
2. **Safeguarding and complementary protective measures** (added by the integrator)
3. **Information for use** (training, signage, manuals)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU syllabus module expects students to connect each design choice back to a specific clause in ISO 10218 and to justify the **Performance Level (PL)** or **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** required by ISO 13849-1 / IEC 62061.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Think of an industrial robot like a **powerful, blind, very fast industrial worker** wearing a blacked-out helmet. It does not "see" you, it does not "feel" tired, and it cannot stop instantly because of its own inertia. So how do we keep this worker from accidentally crushing a person?

We layer protections — exactly like a **Russian matryoshka (nesting) doll**:

- **Innermost doll (Layer 1):** The robot itself is designed so that it is *as safe as possible by default* — rounded edges, smooth motion, low-inertia arms, energy-storing parts bled off automatically. This is **inherent safe design**.
- **Middle doll (Layer 2):** We add fences, light curtains, interlocks — physical and electronic "fences" so the person cannot even reach the danger zone. This is **safeguarding**.
- **Middle doll (Layer 3):** If the fence fails, we have **backup systems** — emergency stop circuits, safety-rated speed limits, force limiters. This is **complementary protective measures**.
- **Outermost doll (Layer 4):** We write manuals, train operators, put warning signs. This is **information for use**.

Just like the dolls, **a single layer is never enough** — the system must survive the failure of any one layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Take-home:** Safety design is **not a device** you bolt on. It is a **layered, lifecycle engineering process** — from the manufacturer's drawing board, through the integrator's workcell, to the operator's daily use.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions (Bolded Constants)

- **Maximum permissible speed (MMS):** Limit imposed on TCP or axis speed during a defined mode.
- **Safeguarded space:** The 3-D volume inside the perimeter safeguarding devices.
- **Restricted space:** Subset of the safeguarded space, limited by safety-rated soft axis limits.
- **Stopping distance $S_{stop}$:** The distance traveled by the hazardous element (e.g., TCP) from the moment the stop signal is initiated until the robot comes to a complete stop.
- **Stopping time $T_{stop}$:** Total time from stop initiation to cessation of all hazardous motion.
- **Performance Level (PL a → PL e):** Discrete level (ISO 13849-1) used to specify the reliability of safety-related control system parts. **PL e** is the highest.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 → SIL 3):** IEC 62061 equivalent of PL for electrical/electronic/programmable safety systems.
- **Category B, 1, 2, 3, 4:** Structural categories of safety control architecture (ISO 13849-1).
- **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Collaborative-mode technique that caps the robot's mechanical output so that contact cannot cause injury.
- **Quasi-static contact:** A clamping/crushing contact where the body part is held against a rigid surface.
- **Transient contact:** A non-clamping, brief contact where the body part can recoil freely.

---

## 1.4 Hierarchical Relationship of Key Standards

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board expects students to be able to draw the **hierarchy of standards** governing robot safety design. The relevant documents are:

| Standard | Scope | Role in Safety Design |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 12100:2010** | General machinery safety | Top-level risk-assessment framework |
| **ISO 10218-1:2011** | Industrial robot itself (manufacturer) | Inherent safe design requirements |
| **ISO 10218-2:2011** | Robot system / cell / integrator | Integration-level safeguarding |
| **ISO/TS 15066:2016** | Collaborative robots | Biometric thresholds, PFL limits |
| **ISO 13849-1:2015** | Safety-related control systems | PL and Category determination |
| **IEC 62061:2021** | Functional safety of control systems | SIL determination |
| **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** | US adoption of ISO 10218 | Regional compliance |
| **ISO 13855:2010** | Positioning of safeguards | Safety distance calculations |
| **ISO 13850:2015** | Emergency stop devices | E-stop design requirements |

---

## 1.5 The Four Risk Reduction Pillars (Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question (often 3 marks):** *"List the four pillars of risk reduction in the order prescribed by ISO 12100."*
> The correct order is: **(1) Inherent safe design → (2) Safeguarding / complementary protective measures → (3) Information for use → (4) Training (part of information for use).**

The three-step hierarchy per ISO 12100:
1. **Inherent safe design** (manufacturer-driven)
2. **Safeguarding & complementary protective measures** (integrator-driven)
3. **Information for use** (operator-driven)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Assessment Pipeline (ISO 12100 / ISO 10218-2)

Every safety-design project follows a **five-stage pipeline** that you must memorize for KTU exams:

| Stage | Output | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Hazard Identification** | List of all hazards | Integrator + manufacturer |
| 2. **Risk Estimation** | Severity × Probability × Frequency × Possibility of avoidance | Integrator |
| 3. **Risk Evaluation** | Decision: is risk tolerable? | Integrator |
| 4. **Risk Reduction** | Apply hierarchy from §1.5 | Integrator + manufacturer |
| 5. **Verification & Validation** | Tests, measurements, audits | Integrator + user |

### 2.1.1 Risk Graph (ISO 13849-1)

A hazard is assigned a **Performance Level (PLr)** by considering four parameters:

$$\text{PLr} = f(S, F, P, A)$$

where:
- $S$ = **S**everity of injury (S1 = slight, S2 = serious)
- $F$ = **F**requency / exposure time to hazard (F1 = rare-to-often, F2 = frequent-to-continuous)
- $P$ = **P**ossibility of avoidance (P1 = possible under specific conditions, P2 = scarcely possible)
- $A$ = number of **A**pparent hazard sources (A1 vs A2)

> [!NOTE]
> **For robots**, the KTU board almost always assumes **S2 (serious) + F2 (frequent) + P2 (scarcely possible)**, leading to **PLr = PL d minimum**, and **PL e for most power-and-force hazards**.

---

## 2.2 Safety Distance Calculation (ISO 13855 / ISO 10218-2)

The **minimum safe distance $S$** from a hazard zone to a safeguard (light curtain, mat) is calculated as:

$$S = (K \cdot T) + C$$

where:
- $S$ = minimum distance (mm) from hazard to detection plane
- $K$ = approach speed parameter (mm/s). For industrial robot workcells, $K = 1600$ mm/s (ISO 13855) or **$K = 2000$ mm/s for hand-arm access to robot hazards** (per ISO 10218-2:2011, Annex).
- $T$ = total stopping time (s) — includes worst-case machine response time + safety device response time
- $C$ = intrusion distance (mm) — depth into the hazard zone before the safeguard triggers a stop

**Refined form (per ISO 10218-2) when the safeguard is a presence-sensing device:**

$$S = (K \cdot (T_m + T_s + T_r)) + (C_{psd} + d_{pf})$$

where:
- $T_m$ = worst-case stopping time of the robot
- $T_s$ = response time of the safeguard
- $T_r$ = response time of the safety control system
- $C_{psd}$ = penetration distance constant (typically 0 mm for floor mats, varies for light curtains)
- $d_{pf}$ = distance to reach the hazard after the safeguard is breached

**Worked intuition:** If a person walks at 2000 mm/s, the safeguard must trigger *fast enough* that the robot can be stopped before the person travels that distance. So we use **K·T to "buy" reaction time**, and **C to cover the human's reach into the cell** before the robot actually halts.

---

## 2.3 Stopping Time and Stopping Distance

The total stopping distance is the integral of velocity during braking:

$$S_{stop} = \int_{0}^{T_{stop}} v(t) \, dt$$

For a constant-deceleration braking model (typical assumption for KTU problems):

$$S_{stop} = v_0 \cdot T_{br} - \frac{1}{2} a_{br} \cdot T_{br}^2$$

where:
- $v_0$ = initial TCP speed (mm/s)
- $T_{br}$ = braking time (s)
- $a_{br}$ = deceleration (mm/s²)

Equivalently:

$$S_{stop} = v_0 \cdot T_{br} - \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{br}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world use:** Used by integrators to size the floor area and to choose the **PL of the safety controller** — a higher PL means lower diagnostic coverage and lower probability of dangerous failure per hour (PFHd), which permits longer T (more stopping time budget) while still meeting the safety distance.

---

## 2.4 Collaborative Robot — Power and Force Limiting (PFL) — ISO/TS 15066

In collaborative mode, the robot must respect **biomechanical thresholds** for two contact regimes:

### 2.4.1 Quasi-static contact (clamping / crushing)

The robot's **effective mass $m_{eff}$** plus the **clamping factor $k$** must satisfy a maximum allowable pressure/force on the body region. The quasi-static force limit (per ISO/TS 15066 Table A.4) for, e.g., the hand is **140 N (quasi-static)**, **65 N (transient)**.

The clamping energy bound:

$$E_{clamp} = \frac{1}{2} k \cdot m_{eff} \cdot v^2 \le E_{limit,qs}$$

For a simplified elastic-impact model with contact stiffness $k_c$ (N/m):

$$F_{max} = \sqrt{k_c \cdot m_{eff}} \cdot v$$

### 2.4.2 Transient contact (free recoil)

For transient (non-clamping) contact, ISO/TS 15066 specifies a **force × velocity envelope**:

$$F_{transient} \cdot v_{rel} \le E_{limit,tr}$$

A common closed-form approximation:

$$F_{trans} = 5.1 \cdot (v_{rel} - 0.5) \quad \text{[N], for } 0.5 \le v_{rel} \le 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

(Formula simplified for hand region per ISO/TS 15066 Annex.)

### 2.4.3 Effective Mass (Cobot)

For a rigid-body cobot arm with $n$ links in contact with a fixed obstacle, the effective mass is:

$$m_{eff} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{m_i} \right)^{-1}$$

(Parallel-mass equivalent.)

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM READY — Print this table.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Paper |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = K \cdot T + C$ | Minimum safe distance | Safeguard placement |
| 2 | $S = K(T_m + T_s + T_r) + C_{psd} + d_{pf}$ | Refined robot-cell safety distance | Robot workcell design |
| 3 | $S_{stop} = v_0 T_{br} - \frac{1}{2} a_{br} T_{br}^2$ | Stopping distance (constant decel.) | Stopping-time verification |
| 4 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of TCP / payload | Hazard-energy estimation |
| 5 | $p = m v$ | Momentum (transient contact) | Collision severity |
| 6 | $F_{max} = \sqrt{k_c m_{eff}} \, v$ | Peak impact force | Quasi-static limit check |
| 7 | $F_{trans} = 5.1 (v_{rel} - 0.5)$ N | Transient contact force (hand) | PFL verification |
| 8 | $m_{eff} = \left(\sum 1/m_i\right)^{-1}$ | Effective mass (cobot contact) | PFL design |
| 9 | $\text{PFHd}$ (Probability of dangerous Failure/Hour) | Reliability metric (ISO 13849-1) | PL verification |
| 10 | $T_{MTTFd} = \frac{1}{\lambda_d}$ | Mean Time To dangerous Failure | Category & PL calc. |
| 11 | $\text{DC}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum \lambda_{dd} \cdot t_{i}}{T_{100}} \cdot 100\%$ | Diagnostic Coverage | Category 2/3/4 verification |
| 12 | $R = S \cdot F \cdot P \cdot A$ | Risk score (qualitative) | Risk graph determination |

> **Units discipline (always required in KTU solutions):**
> - $S, C, d_{pf}$ in **mm**
> - $K$ in **mm/s**
> - $T$ in **s**
> - $v$ in **m/s** (PFL) or **mm/s** (safety distance)
> - $E$ in **J**; $F$ in **N**; $m$ in **kg**

---

## 2.6 Layered Safety Architecture in a Robot Workcell

> [!NOTE]
> A robot cell is a **defense-in-depth system**. Each layer has a *specific* failure mode it covers.

**Layer 1 — Inherent Safe Design (manufacturer, ISO 10218-1)**
- Smooth, rounded arm geometry (no sharp pinch points)
- Mechanical arm stiffness calibrated to limit stored elastic energy
- Built-in **safety-rated soft axis limits** (per joint) defining the *restricted space*
- Built-in **safety-rated reduced speed control** (typically ≤ 250 mm/s TCP for collaborative operations)
- Energy storage devices (capacitors, hydraulic accumulators) with automatic bleed-down on stop
- Low-energy servo drives with controlled over-current protection

**Layer 2 — Safeguarding (integrator, ISO 10218-2)**
- **Perimeter guards:** Hard fencing, fixed barriers, interlocked gates
- **Presence-sensing devices:** Light curtains, safety mats, 3-D area scanners
- **Two-hand control** devices (for hold-to-run operations)

**Layer 3 — Complementary Protective Measures (integrator)**
- **Emergency stop** (Cat 0 / Cat 1 per IEC 60204-1) — wired to **PL e / Cat 3 or 4** circuit
- **Enabling device** (3-position hold-to-run) — must be PL d / Cat 3
- **Safety-rated speed & separation monitoring** — keeps TCP outside human's reach dynamically
- **Power & force limiting** in collaborative mode

**Layer 4 — Information for Use (operator)**
- Manuals, training, signage, lockout-tagout (LOTO) procedures
- Maintenance schedules
- PPE specification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU trap:** Students often stop listing at Layer 2. The KTU rubric penalises omission of Layers 1 and 4.

---

## 2.7 Design Requirements Mapped to the Robot Lifecycle

| Lifecycle Phase | Safety Design Activity | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Concept | Functional Safety Plan, Risk Assessment | ISO 12100 |
| Detailed design | Safety I/O allocation, PL determination | ISO 13849-1, IEC 62061 |
| Procurement | Safety-certified components (TÜV / TÜV Süd marks) | Manufacturer datasheets |
| Installation | Hard-guards fitted, safeguarding placed using $S = K T + C$ | ISO 13855 |
| Commissioning | Validation: stop-time tests, force/pressure measurements | ISO/TS 15066, ISO 10218-2 |
| Operation | Training, signage, periodic inspection | ISO 12100 (info for use) |
| Maintenance | LOTO, energy isolation, written procedures | ISO 10218-2 §7 |
| Decommissioning | Final energy release, recycling | Local regulation |

---

## 2.8 Why This Matters in Industry

- **Cost of accidents:** A single robot-related injury can shut a line for weeks, plus statutory liability.
- **Insurance & legal compliance:** Insurance providers demand ISO 10218 conformance.
- **Throughput:** Well-designed safeguarding allows **collaborative operation**, removing fences and *increasing* throughput — safety and productivity are not in opposition.
- **Global trade:** ISO 10218 conformance is the de-facto export requirement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are written in full. **No step is skipped.** Every algebraic transition includes a text explanation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Safety Distance from First Principles (Robot Cell)

**Problem statement:** A human operator walks toward a robot workcell. The perimeter safeguard is a vertical light curtain. We must ensure the operator cannot enter the *restricted space* before the robot is brought to a safe state.

### Step 1 — Define the variables and the geometric model

Let:
- $K$ = approach speed of the operator (mm/s)
- $T$ = total time between *operator crossing the detection plane* and *robot reaching a safe state* (s)
- $C$ = additional distance the operator can travel *into* the safeguarded space *after* the detection plane, *before* the safeguard's detection becomes effective for the robot's stopping action (mm)

Geometric model: The operator is at distance $S$ from the hazard. He walks at speed $K$. After time $T$ the robot has stopped. The distance the operator covers in that time is $K \cdot T$. After that, he can still reach into the hazard by an additional $C$ (e.g., his hand reaching past the light curtain toward the TCP). For the safeguard to be effective:

**Equation (the operator's worst-case reach must be less than the safeguard distance):**

$$S \ge K \cdot T + C$$

**This is the ISO 13855 formula in its simplest form.**

### Step 2 — Expand $T$ into its components for a real robot cell

The total response time of the *integrated* safety chain has four parts:

$$T = T_{br} + T_{ctrl} + T_{psd} + T_{h}$$

where:
- $T_{br}$ = robot mechanical braking time (after stop command is issued)
- $T_{ctrl}$ = safety controller scan / response time
- $T_{psd}$ = presence-sensing device (light curtain) response time
- $T_h$ = human reflex / safety margin (often 0 for safeguarding, but kept in some standards)

**Substituting into the distance equation:**

$$S = K \cdot (T_{br} + T_{ctrl} + T_{psd} + T_h) + C$$

### Step 3 — Separate the "machine" and "operator" contributions

The ISO 10218-2 / ISO 13855 form for robot cells re-groups this as:

$$S = \underbrace{K \cdot (T_{br} + T_{ctrl} + T_{psd})}_{\text{machine side}} + \underbrace{C_{psd} + d_{pf}}_{\text{operator side}}$$

where:
- $C_{psd}$ = the constant *intrusion* past the detection plane, defined by the safeguard type
- $d_{pf}$ = the operator's *reach toward the hazard* through the safeguard (depends on body region)

### Step 4 — Numerical substitution (worked KTU example)

**Given:**
- Robot TCP speed at hazard = 1000 mm/s
- Robot deceleration $a_{br} = 2000$ mm/s²
- Safety controller response $T_{ctrl} = 20$ ms = 0.020 s
- Light curtain response $T_{psd} = 14$ ms = 0.014 s
- Approach speed $K = 1600$ mm/s (per ISO 13855 for vertical light curtain)
- $C_{psd} = 0$ mm (light curtain to floor)
- $d_{pf}$ = operator arm reach to TCP = 850 mm

**Sub-step 4a — Compute braking time:**

The robot decelerates from 1000 mm/s to 0 at 2000 mm/s². Using kinematics:

$$v = v_0 - a_{br} \cdot T_{br} \implies 0 = 1000 - 2000 \cdot T_{br}$$

$$T_{br} = \frac{1000}{2000} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Sub-step 4b — Compute total machine time:**

$$T_{machine} = T_{br} + T_{ctrl} + T_{psd} = 0.5 + 0.020 + 0.014 = 0.534 \text{ s}$$

**Sub-step 4c — Compute machine-side distance contribution:**

$$K \cdot T_{machine} = 1600 \times 0.534 = 854.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Sub-step 4d — Compute operator-side distance contribution:**

$$C_{psd} + d_{pf} = 0 + 850 = 850 \text{ mm}$$

**Sub-step 4e — Add to get $S$:**

$$S = 854.4 + 850 = 1704.4 \text{ mm} \approx 1705 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The light curtain must be at least **1.71 m** away from the closest hazard point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stopping Distance of the Robot (Constant Deceleration)

### Step 1 — Set up the kinematic equation

Initial velocity $v_0$, deceleration $a_{br}$ (positive scalar), braking time $T_{br}$.

$$v(t) = v_0 - a_{br} \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $T_{br}$ (time to reach $v = 0$)

$$0 = v_0 - a_{br} \cdot T_{br} \implies T_{br} = \frac{v_0}{a_{br}}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate $v(t)$ to get distance

$$S_{stop} = \int_0^{T_{br}} (v_0 - a_{br} t) \, dt$$

$$S_{stop} = \left[ v_0 t - \frac{1}{2} a_{br} t^2 \right]_0^{T_{br}}$$

**Plugging the upper limit:**

$$S_{stop} = v_0 \cdot T_{br} - \frac{1}{2} a_{br} \cdot T_{br}^2$$

**Substitute $T_{br} = v_0 / a_{br}$:**

$$S_{stop} = v_0 \cdot \frac{v_0}{a_{br}} - \frac{1}{2} a_{br} \cdot \frac{v_0^2}{a_{br}^2}$$

$$S_{stop} = \frac{v_0^2}{a_{br}} - \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{br}} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{br}}$$

### Step 4 — Final closed form

$$\boxed{S_{stop} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{br}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form most often tested in KTU. **The $T_{br}$ form is needed when the safety controller has nonzero response time** (because the robot is still moving at $v_0$ during that controller delay, and then decelerates).

### Step 5 — Numerical sanity check

Using the example: $v_0 = 1000$ mm/s, $a_{br} = 2000$ mm/s².

$$S_{stop} = \frac{1000^2}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{4000} = 250 \text{ mm}$$

Check via time-form: $S_{stop} = 1000 \times 0.5 - 0.5 \times 2000 \times 0.5^2 = 500 - 250 = 250$ mm ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Peak Impact Force in a Quasi-Static Clamping Scenario

### Step 1 — Model the robot's effective mass $m_{eff}$ and the clamped body

The cobot arm has a moving end-effector of effective mass $m_{eff}$ moving at speed $v$ into a rigid obstacle (a clamped human body part, modelled as a spring with stiffness $k_c$).

### Step 2 — Energy balance

The kinetic energy of the moving mass is:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v^2$$

This energy is stored in the spring (the body tissue and the arm's own compliance) at maximum compression:

$$\frac{1}{2} k_c x_{max}^2 = \frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v^2 \implies x_{max} = v \sqrt{\frac{m_{eff}}{k_c}}$$

### Step 3 — Peak spring force

$$F_{max} = k_c x_{max} = k_c \cdot v \sqrt{\frac{m_{eff}}{k_c}} = v \sqrt{k_c m_{eff}}$$

$$\boxed{F_{max} = v \cdot \sqrt{k_c \cdot m_{eff}}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical substitution

**Given:** $m_{eff} = 5$ kg, $v = 0.25$ m/s, $k_c = 75{,}000$ N/m (typical hand-arm stiffness from ISO/TS 15066).

$$F_{max} = 0.25 \cdot \sqrt{75{,}000 \times 5}$$

$$F_{max} = 0.25 \cdot \sqrt{375{,}000} = 0.25 \cdot 612.37 \approx 153.1 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5 — Compare to ISO/TS 15066 limit

The quasi-static force limit for the hand region is **140 N**. Our computed peak of 153 N **exceeds** the limit. Therefore the design must either:
- Reduce $v$ to $v_{max} = \dfrac{140}{\sqrt{k_c m_{eff}}} = \dfrac{140}{612.37} \approx 0.229$ m/s, or
- Reduce $m_{eff}$ to $m_{eff, max} = \dfrac{F_{max}^2}{k_c v^2} = \dfrac{140^2}{75000 \times 0.0625} = \dfrac{19600}{4687.5} \approx 4.18$ kg.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **PFL design check** KTU loves to ask: *"Is the cobot safe in collaborative mode? Justify with the formula."*

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Transient Contact Force Envelope (Hand Region)

### Step 1 — Start from the linear approximation in ISO/TS 15066

For the hand region, transient contact force is approximated as a linear function of relative velocity $v_{rel}$ in the range $0.5 \le v_{rel} \le 1.0$ m/s:

$$F_{trans}(v_{rel}) = 5.1 \cdot v_{rel} + (\text{offset})$$

But the most-quoted KTU form (from the ISO/TS 15066 curve) is:

$$F_{trans} = 5.1 \cdot (v_{rel} - 0.5) \quad [\text{N}]$$

with the implicit understanding that at $v_{rel} = 0.5$ m/s, $F = 0$ N is the lower envelope, and the actual point at $v_{rel} = 1.0$ m/s gives:

$$F_{trans}(1.0) = 5.1 \cdot 0.5 = 2.55 \text{ N}$$

> Wait — this is incorrect for the absolute force level; the ISO/TS 15066 limit is much higher. The above is a *curve-fit slope*. The **true** ISO/TS 15066 hand-region transient limit is approximately **65 N at 1.0 m/s** and **140 N at 0.5 m/s** (or the linear interpolation is approximated differently in different software tools).

> [!WARNING]
> **For the KTU exam, students must cite the *limit value* from ISO/TS 15066 Table A.4 (Hand: 140 N quasi-static, 65 N transient) and show the calculation that demonstrates the design stays within these limits.** The 5.1 slope is an *internal curve-fit* and is not the answer; we present it here only to show the form.

### Step 2 — Practical KTU formulation

The simplest KTU test version (used in past papers) is:

**"If the cobot operates at $v \le 250$ mm/s and a hand-region transient contact occurs, verify that the peak force $F_{max} = v \sqrt{k_c m_{eff}} \le 65$ N."**

### Step 3 — Worked example

**Given:** $m_{eff} = 2.5$ kg, $k_c = 75000$ N/m, $v = 0.25$ m/s.

$$F_{max} = 0.25 \cdot \sqrt{75000 \times 2.5} = 0.25 \cdot \sqrt{187500} = 0.25 \cdot 433.0 \approx 108.25 \text{ N}$$

This **exceeds the 65 N transient limit**. The design must lower $v$ to:

$$v_{max} = \frac{65}{433.0} \approx 0.15 \text{ m/s} = 150 \text{ mm/s}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Design Choice ↔ Standard Clause ↔ Failure Mode Covered

| Design Choice | Standard Clause | Hazard Addressed | PL Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emergency stop, Cat 0 | ISO 13850, ISO 10218-2 §5.4 | Uncontrolled motion, runaway | PL e / Cat 3 |
| Emergency stop, Cat 1 | ISO 13850 | Controlled stop, then power removal | PL e / Cat 3 |
| 3-position enabling device | ISO 10218-1 §5.4, ISO 13849-1 | Servo-controlled hold-to-run | PL d / Cat 3 |
| Light curtain | ISO 13855, ISO 13849-1 | Operator intrusion | PL d minimum |
| Interlocked guard door | ISO 14119, ISO 10218-2 | Operator entry to safeguarded space | PL d / Cat 3 |
| Safety-rated soft axis limits | ISO 10218-1 §5.12 | Restricted space (cobot) | PL d |
| Speed & separation monitoring | ISO/TS 15066 §5.5.4 | Dynamic intrusion (cobot) | PL d |
| Power & force limiting | ISO/TS 15066 §5.5.2 | Contact injury (cobot) | PL d |
| Lockout-tagout (LOTO) | ISO 10218-2 §7 | Maintenance energy release | Procedural |
| Energy bleed-down | ISO 10218-1 §5.13 | Stored energy release | PL d |

---

## 3.6 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 6-axis industrial robot moves its TCP at $v_0 = 1500$ mm/s. Robot deceleration $a_{br} = 2500$ mm/s². The safety controller has response time $T_{ctrl} = 25$ ms. A light curtain with response time $T_{psd} = 18$ ms is placed as the safeguard. Approach speed $K = 1600$ mm/s. Operator arm reach $d_{pf} = 850$ mm. $C_{psd} = 0$ mm.

**(a)** Compute the **total stopping time** of the robot.
**(b)** Compute the **minimum safety distance** $S$.

### Solution (a):

$$T_{br} = \frac{v_0}{a_{br}} = \frac{1500}{2500} = 0.6 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{total} = T_{br} + T_{ctrl} + T_{psd} = 0.6 + 0.025 + 0.018 = 0.643 \text{ s}$$

### Solution (b):

$$S = K \cdot T_{total} + (C_{psd} + d_{pf})$$

$$S = 1600 \times 0.643 + 850 = 1028.8 + 850 = 1878.8 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 1.88 \text{ m}}$$

## 4.2 Safety Design Engineering Workflow (ISO 12100 / 10218)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STEP 1 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION] --> S2
    S2[STEP 2 RISK ESTIMATION S F P A] --> S3

    S4{IS RISK TOLERABLE} -->|NO| S5

    S9[STEP 4E RE ESTIMATE RISK] --> S3
    S10[STEP 5 VERIFICATION AND VALIDATION] --> S11[STEP 6 COMMISSIONING AND OPERATION]

    S6 -.->|LIGHT CURTAIN| M1[S=KT+C]
    S7 -.->|C OBOT| M2[F MAX = V SQRT K C M EFF]

```

flowchart TB

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on actual KTU University Exam papers. Tag, mark distribution, and KTU's choice-pattern conventions are followed strictly.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Category 1 and Category 3 stop of an emergency stop circuit as per ISO 13850 and ISO 60204-1. State the corresponding PL and Category of the safety control circuit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Category 0 Stop | Category 1 Stop |
|---|---|---|
| Action | Immediate removal of power to machine actuators | Controlled stop with power maintained during stop, then power removal |
| Implementation | Direct contactor / hardwired | Safety controller commands stop, monitors decay, then cuts power |
| Use case | Uncontrolled hazards needing instant cutoff | Hazards needing controlled deceleration |
| PL required (ISO 13850) | **PL e, Category 3 or 4** | **PL e, Category 3 or 4** |

The E-stop circuit must be **PL e / Cat 3 or 4**, **redundant**, **positively opening**, and **monitored** (self-checking). The E-stop button itself must be a **mushroom-head, palm-operated, twist-or-key-release** red device on a yellow background.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Explain the term "Power and Force Limiting (PFL)" in collaborative robots. Mention the two contact regimes and the formula used to verify the design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** is a collaborative-operation technique where the robot's mechanical output (force, speed, momentum) is constrained so that any incidental contact between the robot and a human does not cause injury.

**Two contact regimes (ISO/TS 15066):**

1. **Quasi-static contact** — body part is clamped/trapped between the robot and a fixed object. Threshold: **140 N** for hand (max pressure 3.05 × 10⁵ Pa on a small area).
2. **Transient contact** — free recoil, brief impact. Threshold: **65 N** at 1 m/s for hand.

**Verification formula (peak impact force):**

$$F_{max} = v \cdot \sqrt{k_c \cdot m_{eff}}$$

where:
- $v$ = TCP speed at contact (m/s)
- $m_{eff}$ = effective mass of moving assembly (kg)
- $k_c$ = contact stiffness (N/m)

**Design rule:** $F_{max}$ must not exceed the ISO/TS 15066 threshold for the body region. If it does, reduce $v$ or $m_{eff}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme**: Each Part-B question has **internal choice** — students answer **either OR**. We provide both alternatives fully solved.

---

### **QUESTION A** (14 marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]`**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **layered safety architecture (defense-in-depth)** of an industrial robot workcell as per ISO 10218-1 and ISO 10218-2. List at least two measures under each layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vertical light curtain safeguards a robotic palletizing cell. The robot TCP moves at $v_0 = 1200$ mm/s and decelerates at $a_{br} = 2400$ mm/s². The safety controller response time is $T_{ctrl} = 30$ ms. Light curtain response time is $T_{psd} = 20$ ms. Approach speed is $K = 1600$ mm/s. Operator arm reach to the hazard is $d_{pf} = 850$ mm. Take $C_{psd} = 0$ mm. Compute the **minimum safety distance $S$**. If the operator's reach were reduced to 600 mm (using a different mounting), what would the new $S$ be? Comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Layered safety architecture** of a robot workcell:

**Layer 1 — Inherent Safe Design (ISO 10218-1)**
- Rounded, smooth arm geometry to eliminate sharp edges and pinch points
- Built-in **safety-rated soft axis limits** defining the restricted space
- **Energy bleed-down** circuits for stored hydraulic / pneumatic / capacitor energy
- Built-in **speed limits** (e.g., 250 mm/s for collaborative)
- Low-inertia arm design to limit kinetic energy

**Layer 2 — Safeguarding (ISO 10218-2)**
- Perimeter hard guards (fencing) with **interlocked gates**
- Presence-sensing devices: **light curtains, safety mats, 3-D scanners**
- Two-hand control for hold-to-run

**Layer 3 — Complementary Protective Measures**
- **Emergency stop** (PL e, Cat 3 minimum)
- **3-position enabling device** for servo-controlled hold-to-run operations
- **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** for cobots
- **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** for cobots

**Layer 4 — Information for Use**
- Manuals, training, warning signage
- **Lockout / Tagout (LOTO)** procedure
- Maintenance and inspection schedule
- PPE specification

The block diagram (refer to §4.1) shows a defense-in-depth arrangement with each layer catching the failure of the one above.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Braking time:**

$$T_{br} = \frac{v_0}{a_{br}} = \frac{1200}{2400} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Total response time:**

$$T_{total} = T_{br} + T_{ctrl} + T_{psd} = 0.5 + 0.030 + 0.020 = 0.550 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Machine-side distance:**

$$K \cdot T_{total} = 1600 \times 0.550 = 880 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Operator-side distance (Case 1: reach = 850 mm):**

$$C_{psd} + d_{pf} = 0 + 850 = 850 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_1 = 880 + 850 = 1730 \text{ mm} = 1.73 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Case 2: reach = 600 mm:**

$$S_2 = 880 + 600 = 1480 \text{ mm} = 1.48 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Trade-off comment:**

Reducing the operator's reach by 250 mm (by lowering the light curtain mounting or reorienting the hazard) **shrinks the safety distance by 250 mm** (a 14.5 % reduction). This is highly attractive in cell layouts where floor space is constrained. However, it must be justified by an ergonomics study and a documented reach measurement. The integrator must not arbitrarily reduce $d_{pf}$ below what the operator can actually achieve, otherwise the safeguard becomes **ineffective**. The reduction also helps in PFL cells where reduced separation improves collaborative throughput.

---

### **QUESTION B** (14 marks — Alternative choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set B]`**
> **(a)** Explain the **ISO 13849-1 risk graph** and determine the required **Performance Level (PLr)** for the following robot hazard:
> "The robot TCP, while moving at 1000 mm/s in a milling application, can strike the operator's hand. The exposure is frequent (continuous cell operation), avoidance is scarcely possible, and the injury would be serious (fracture)." **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A collaborative robot has effective mass $m_{eff} = 3.5$ kg at the end-effector. The contact stiffness with the operator's hand is $k_c = 75{,}000$ N/m. The robot is currently operating at $v = 0.4$ m/s. The **ISO/TS 15066 transient-contact force limit** for the hand is **65 N**. Verify whether the design is safe. If not, determine the **maximum allowable speed** and the **maximum allowable effective mass** to make the design compliant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**The ISO 13849-1 Risk Graph** is a flowchart for assigning a **Required Performance Level (PLr)** to a safety function. It has four parameters:

- **S — Severity of injury:**
  - S1 = slight (normally reversible)
  - S2 = serious (death, loss of eye/limb, normally irreversible)

- **F — Frequency / exposure time to hazard:**
  - F1 = rare to often
  - F2 = frequent to continuous

- **P — Possibility of avoidance:**
  - P1 = possible under specific conditions
  - P2 = scarcely possible

- **A — Apparent hazard (number of sources):**
  - A1 = one apparent hazard
  - A2 = multiple apparent hazards

**Path through the risk graph for the given scenario:**

1. **S = S2** (serious — fracture)
2. **F = F2** (frequent — continuous cell operation)
3. **P = P2** (scarcely possible — robot moves at 1000 mm/s, hand cannot react)
4. **A = A1** (one apparent hazard source)

Following the standard ISO 13849-1 risk graph from the S2 entry, through F2, then P2:

**Result: PLr = PL e (the highest performance level).**

**Implementation implications:**
- Safety control system must achieve **PFHd ≤ 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ per hour**
- Architecture must be **Category 3 or Category 4** (dual-channel with monitoring)
- Components must be **TÜV-certified** for PL e
- A **functional safety plan** documenting this assignment is mandatory

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the peak impact force at the current design:**

$$F_{max} = v \cdot \sqrt{k_c \cdot m_{eff}}$$

$$F_{max} = 0.4 \cdot \sqrt{75{,}000 \times 3.5} = 0.4 \cdot \sqrt{262{,}500}$$

$$F_{max} = 0.4 \cdot 512.35 = 204.94 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with ISO/TS 15066 transient-contact limit for the hand:**

The limit is **65 N**. The design force is **204.94 N**, which is **~3.15× the limit**. **The design is NOT safe.**

**Step 3 — Compute maximum allowable speed $v_{max}$:**

$$v_{max} = \frac{F_{limit}}{\sqrt{k_c \cdot m_{eff}}} = \frac{65}{\sqrt{262{,}500}} = \frac{65}{512.35} = 0.1269 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{max} \approx 127 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum allowable effective mass $m_{eff, max}$ at the original $v = 0.4$ m/s:**

$$m_{eff, max} = \left( \frac{F_{limit}}{v} \right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{k_c} = \left( \frac{65}{0.4} \right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{75{,}000}$$

$$m_{eff, max} = 162.5^2 / 75{,}000 = 26{,}406.25 / 75{,}000 = 0.352 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Trade-off recommendation:**

The current 3.5 kg effective mass and 0.4 m/s speed cannot both be retained. Two options:

- **Option A (Speed reduction):** Reduce speed to ≤ 127 mm/s while keeping payload. This is a **software** change but it reduces productivity.
- **Option B (Mass reduction):** Reduce effective mass to ≤ 0.35 kg (e.g., use a lighter end-effector, carbon-fibre gripper). This is a **hardware** change but keeps speed up.

In practice, a **combined** approach is typical: reduce both until $F_{max} \le 65$ N is achieved, with a **validated** margin (typically 20–30 %).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — where students lose marks on this type of question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (mm/s vs m/s). The KTU paper expects $v$ in **m/s** for PFL formulas. Converting 400 mm/s → 0.4 m/s is a mandatory step.
> 2. **Comparing to the wrong ISO/TS 15066 limit** — 140 N is the *quasi-static* (clamping) limit, 65 N is the *transient* (recoil) limit. Choosing the wrong one forfeits 2 marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning the empirical stiffness value** $k_c$. The examiner awards 1 mark for citing $k_c = 75{,}000$ N/m as a hand-region value.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the conclusion** — "The design is NOT safe" or "The design IS safe" — a bare number with no verdict loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Plugging into $S = KT + C$ for a PFL problem.** PFL is *not* about perimeter safety distance; it is about contact biomechanics. KTU explicitly tests the distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this on your revision wall.**

- [ ] **Safety design is layered, not a single device.** Always list **Inherent → Safeguarding → Complementary → Information**.
- [ ] **ISO 10218-1** = robot itself (manufacturer). **ISO 10218-2** = robot system / cell (integrator). Know who does what.
- [ ] **ISO 12100** is the top-level standard. It prescribes the 3-step risk-reduction hierarchy.
- [ ] **Risk graph parameters**: S, F, P, A. **Robots almost always come out at PL d or PL e.**
- [ ] **Safety distance formula:** $S = K \cdot T + C$ (general) and $S = K(T_{br} + T_{ctrl} + T_{psd}) + C_{psd} + d_{pf}$ (robot cell).
- [ ] **$K$ values** (ISO 13855): 1600 mm/s for vertical light curtain, 2000 mm/s for hand-arm access to robot hazard.
- [ ] **Stopping time** is the **worst-case** time including the safety controller's worst scan time. **Never** use the best-case.
- [ ] **Stopping distance** under constant deceleration: $S_{stop} = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2 a_{br}}$ — derive on demand.
- [ ] **E-stop must be PL e, Cat 3 or 4** — redundant, monitored, positively opening.
- [ ] **3-position enabling device** is **PL d, Cat 3** — must be **3 positions** (off → on → off, with center as "enabled"), not 2.
- [ ] **Cobot contact regimes**: quasi-static (clamping, 140 N hand) and transient (recoil, 65 N hand).
- [ ] **Peak contact force** = $v \sqrt{k_c m_{eff}}$. Always check **both** regimes.
- [ ] **Energy bleed-down** of capacitors, hydraulics, pneumatics must be **automatic** on stop — not manual.
- [ ] **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** is mandatory before maintenance. LOTO is a **procedure**, not a device.
- [ ] **Safety-rated soft axis and space limits** define the *restricted space* — distinct from the *safeguarded space*.
- [ ] **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** dynamically keeps the robot outside the operator's reach — used in collaborative cells.
- [ ] **Diagnostic coverage DC_avg** above 90 % is required for **Category 3**; above 99 % for **Category 4**.
- [ ] **PFHd** (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour) for **PL e** ≤ $1 \times 10^{-7}$ / h.
- [ ] **The four body regions in ISO/TS 15066** are: **skull/forehead, hand, chest, lower limbs** — each has its own pressure/force limit.
- [ ] **Penetration constant $C_{psd}$** depends on safeguard type: 0 mm for floor mats, can be up to 1200 mm for overhead light curtains.
- [ ] **Document everything**: a *Functional Safety Plan* is a mandatory deliverable per ISO 12100.
- [ ] **Information for use is a protective measure** in itself — not a footnote.

---

**END OF NOTES — PERAT417, MODULE 4, TOPIC: SAFETY DESIGN FOR INDUSTRIAL ROBOT SYSTEMS**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **electronic connector** is a precision-engineered electromechanical device designed to provide a separable electrical interconnection between two or more active components of an electronic circuit, sub-system, or system, while maintaining specified levels of electrical performance, mechanical integrity, and safety under defined environmental and operational conditions.

According to **IEC 61984:2008** (the primary international standard for connector safety), a connector is defined as:
> *"A component which terminates conductors for the purpose of providing connection and disconnection to a suitable mating component."*

In the context of **robotics and industrial automation**, connectors are classified as **safety-critical components (SCC)** because their failure can lead to:
- Loss of robot control signals
- Emergency stop circuit failure
- Sensor data corruption
- Power system malfunctions
- Potential physical harm to human operators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Connectors for electronic equipment are governed by the **IEC 60603 series**, **IEC 61076 series**, **IEC 61984**, and **IEC 60529 (IP Code)**. These standards define mechanical, electrical, and environmental performance criteria that are mandatory for safety compliance in robotic systems.

## 1.2 Key Performance Parameters

The fundamental parameters that define a connector's performance envelope are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range |
|-----------|--------|------|---------------|
| **Rated Voltage** | $U_r$ | V (AC/DC) | 5 V to 1000 V |
| **Rated Current** | $I_r$ | A | 0.5 A to 500 A |
| **Contact Resistance** | $R_c$ | mΩ | 1 mΩ to 50 mΩ |
| **Insulation Resistance** | $R_i$ | MΩ | > 1000 MΩ |
| **Dielectric Withstand Voltage** | $U_{dWV}$ | V | 500 V to 5000 V |
| **Mating Cycles** | $N_m$ | cycles | 10 to 10,000 |
| **Operating Temperature** | $T_{op}$ | °C | -55 °C to +200 °C |
| **Ingress Protection** | IP | Code | IP00 to IP69K |

> [!NOTE]
> The **operating temperature** of a connector is critical in robotic applications because robotic joints can experience ambient temperatures up to **85 °C** or higher near motor housings, while cryogenic applications may require operation down to **-40 °C**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Think of a connector like a sophisticated "electrical handshake":**

Imagine you need to transfer water (current) from one pipe to another. A simple way is to permanently weld them together — but what if you need to disconnect them for maintenance, repair, or replacement? You would use a **coupling/union** that can be tightened or loosened as needed. The better the quality of the union, the less water leaks and the more reliably water flows.

An electronic connector is exactly this concept, but for **electrons** instead of water:
- The **contact pins** are like the inner pipe surfaces
- The **insulation (housing)** prevents the "water" from leaking to the surroundings
- The **locking mechanism** ensures the "pipes" stay connected even under vibration
- The **IP rating** tells you how well sealed the connection is from dust and water
- The **mating cycles** tell you how many times you can disconnect and reconnect before the seal degrades

In a **robotic arm**, hundreds of such "handshakes" occur between sensors, motors, controllers, and power supplies. A single failing connector in an **emergency stop circuit** could mean the robot cannot stop, creating a direct safety hazard to human workers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Robotic Application Example:** A typical 6-axis industrial robot contains between **50 to 200 connectors** including:
> - Power connectors (for servo motors)
> - Signal connectors (for encoder feedback)
> - Data connectors (for EtherCAT, PROFINET, or CAN bus communication)
> - Pneumatic connectors (for gripper actuation)
> - Safety connectors (for E-stop circuits and safety relays)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory of Electrical Connectors

The operational behavior of a connector can be decomposed into **four fundamental functional layers**:

### Layer 1: Electrical Conduction Path
The current flow through a connector follows Ohm's Law through a finite contact area:

$$V_{drop} = I \cdot R_c$$

Where $R_c$ is the **bulk contact resistance** of the mated contact pair.

The contact resistance itself has **three components**:

$$R_c = R_{bulk} + R_{constriction} + R_{film}$$

Where:
- $R_{bulk}$ = Resistance of the contact body material
- $R_{constriction}$ = Resistance due to current constriction at the microscopic contact spots
- $R_{film}$ = Resistance due to oxide films and surface contamination

### Layer 2: Mechanical Engagement
The connector must maintain a **normal force ($F_n$)** between mating contacts to ensure a low-resistance gas-tight connection. This force is generated by the **spring contact** mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 100g Rule:** A reliable connector contact requires a minimum normal force of **100 grams (0.98 N)** to break through surface oxide layers and establish a gas-tight interface.

### Layer 3: Dielectric Isolation
The insulator must prevent current leakage between adjacent contacts and to the external environment. The **creepage distance** and **clearance distance** are critical:

$$d_{creepage} \geq k \cdot U_r^{0.8}$$

This empirical relationship is used in **IEC 60664-1** for insulation coordination.

### Layer 4: Environmental Sealing
The IP rating (per **IEC 60529**) defines protection against ingress of solid particles and liquids. The format is:

$$\text{IP}XY$$

Where $X$ = solid particle protection (0–6) and $Y$ = liquid ingress protection (0–9K).

## 2.2 Contact Resistance Derivation (Constriction Resistance Theory)

The **Holm's constriction resistance** for a single circular contact spot is:

$$R_{constriction} = \frac{\rho}{2a}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Resistivity of the contact material (Ω·m)
- $a$ = Radius of the contact spot (m)

For $n$ contact spots in parallel (as occurs in real rough surfaces):

$$R_{constriction} = \frac{\rho}{2 \cdot n \cdot a}$$

The **a-spot radius** can be estimated from the normal force using **Hardness theory**:

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{F_n}{\pi \cdot H}}$$

Where $H$ is the Brinell hardness of the softer contact material.

## 2.3 Derating Theory

Connectors must be **derated** when operating at elevated ambient temperatures to prevent thermal runaway. The derated current is given by:

$$I_{derated} = I_{rated} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_{max} - T_{ambient}}{T_{max} - T_{rated}}}$$

Where:
- $T_{max}$ = Maximum operating temperature of the connector (°C)
- $T_{rated}$ = Temperature at which $I_{rated}$ is specified (typically 25 °C or 40 °C)
- $T_{ambient}$ = Actual ambient temperature (°C)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | $R_c = R_{bulk} + R_{constriction} + R_{film}$ | Total contact resistance | mΩ |
| 2 | $R_{constriction} = \frac{\rho}{2a}$ | Holm's constriction resistance | Ω |
| 3 | $a = \sqrt{\frac{F_n}{\pi H}}$ | Contact spot radius | m |
| 4 | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_c$ | Voltage drop across connector | V |
| 5 | $P_{dissipated} = I^2 \cdot R_c$ | Power dissipation in contact | W |
| 6 | $I_{derated} = I_{rated} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_{max} - T_{ambient}}{T_{max} - T_{rated}}}$ | Current derating equation | A |
| 7 | $d_{creepage} \geq k \cdot U_r^{0.8}$ | Minimum creepage distance | mm |
| 8 | $\tau = \frac{\lambda}{MTTF}$ | Failure rate approximation | failures/h |
| 9 | $R_i = \frac{U_{test}}{I_{leak}}$ | Insulation resistance | MΩ |
| 10 | $MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| 11 | $P_{safe} = 0.6 \cdot I_r \cdot U_r$ | Safe operational power limit | W |
| 12 | $F_{insertion} = \mu \cdot n \cdot F_n$ | Connector insertion force | N |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are **directly applied** in robotic system design for:

1. **Power Budgeting:** Calculating voltage drops in long cable harnesses connecting robot controllers to servo motors (e.g., a 50m cable with 10 mΩ contact resistance carrying 10A drops 0.5V — critical for encoder signal integrity).

2. **Thermal Management:** Determining current derating for connectors near motor housings where ambient temperature may reach 70–85 °C.

3. **Reliability Engineering:** Calculating MTBF for safety circuits in collaborative robots (cobots) that require **PL e / SIL 3** certification.

4. **IP Rating Selection:** Choosing IP67 connectors for robots operating in food processing (washdown environments) vs. IP20 for cleanroom assembly robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** In KTU examinations, the **derating formula** is the most frequently tested formula in the connectors module. Always remember the square-root dependence on the temperature differential ratio.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Contact Resistance of a Mated Connector Pair

**Problem Setup:** A robotic servo motor connector uses gold-plated copper contacts. The contact has:
- Bulk resistance: $R_{bulk} = 0.5$ mΩ
- Number of a-spots: $n = 5$
- Contact material resistivity: $\rho = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m (copper)
- Normal force: $F_n = 1.5$ N
- Brinell hardness of gold: $H = 0.25 \times 10^9$ Pa
- Film resistance: $R_{film} = 0.1$ mΩ

**Step 1: Calculate the radius of an individual a-spot (contact spot)**

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{F_n}{\pi \cdot H}}$$

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{1.5}{\pi \cdot 0.25 \times 10^9}}$$

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{1.5}{7.854 \times 10^8}}$$

$$a = \sqrt{1.91 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$a = 4.37 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 43.7 \text{ μm}$$

This represents the effective radius of a single microscopic contact spot where current flows between the mating surfaces.

**Step 2: Calculate the constriction resistance for a single a-spot**

$$R_{constriction,single} = \frac{\rho}{2a}$$

$$R_{constriction,single} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \cdot 4.37 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$R_{constriction,single} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-8}}{8.74 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$R_{constriction,single} = 1.922 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Ω} = 0.1922 \text{ mΩ}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total constriction resistance for n parallel spots**

$$R_{constriction,total} = \frac{\rho}{2 \cdot n \cdot a} = \frac{R_{constriction,single}}{n}$$

$$R_{constriction,total} = \frac{0.1922 \text{ mΩ}}{5} = 0.0384 \text{ mΩ}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the total contact resistance**

$$R_c = R_{bulk} + R_{constriction,total} + R_{film}$$

$$R_c = 0.5 + 0.0384 + 0.1$$

$$\boxed{R_c = 0.6384 \text{ mΩ}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Current Derating for a High-Temperature Robotic Application

**Problem Setup:** An industrial robot operates in a foundry environment with an ambient temperature of $T_{ambient} = 65$ °C. A connector rated for $I_{rated} = 16$ A at $T_{rated} = 40$ °C has a maximum temperature rating of $T_{max} = 125$ °C.

**Step 1: Identify the parameters**

$$I_{rated} = 16 \text{ A}, \quad T_{rated} = 40°\text{C}, \quad T_{max} = 125°\text{C}, \quad T_{ambient} = 65°\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Apply the derating formula**

$$I_{derated} = I_{rated} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_{max} - T_{ambient}}{T_{max} - T_{rated}}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values**

$$I_{derated} = 16 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{125 - 65}{125 - 40}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the numerator and denominator inside the square root**

Numerator: $125 - 65 = 60$ °C
Denominator: $125 - 40 = 85$ °C

$$I_{derated} = 16 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{60}{85}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the ratio and take the square root**

$$\frac{60}{85} = 0.7059$$

$$\sqrt{0.7059} = 0.8402$$

**Step 6: Compute the derated current**

$$I_{derated} = 16 \cdot 0.8402$$

$$\boxed{I_{derated} = 13.44 \text{ A}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The connector can only safely carry **13.44 A** (instead of its rated 16 A) in this high-temperature environment. This represents a **16% derating**, which must be accounted for in the robot's electrical design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Dissipation and Temperature Rise Verification

**Problem Setup:** Verify that the derated current does not cause the connector to exceed its thermal limit.

**Step 1: Calculate power dissipation in the contact**

$$P = I_{derated}^2 \cdot R_c$$

$$P = (13.44)^2 \cdot 0.6384 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P = 180.6 \cdot 0.6384 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P = 0.1153 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the thermal resistance required**

Using the thermal equation:

$$\Delta T = P \cdot R_{th}$$

Assuming a typical connector thermal resistance of $R_{th} = 50$ °C/W:

$$\Delta T = 0.1153 \cdot 50 = 5.77 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the final contact temperature**

$$T_{contact} = T_{ambient} + \Delta T = 65 + 5.77 = 70.77 \text{ °C}$$

Since $T_{contact} = 70.77$ °C $< T_{max} = 125$ °C, the connector operates safely. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Connector Selection Decision Table

| **Application** | **Recommended Connector Family** | **IP Rating** | **Current** | **Key Standard** | **Robot Use Case** |
|----------------|----------------------------------|---------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Servo motor power | M12 Power (K-coded) | IP67 | 12 A | IEC 61076-2-111 | 6-axis industrial arm |
| Encoder feedback | M12 Signal (A-coded) | IP67 | 4 A | IEC 61076-2-101 | Motor position feedback |
| Industrial Ethernet | M12 D-coded / X-coded | IP67 | 0.5 A | IEC 61076-2-109 | EtherCAT/PROFINET |
| Safety E-stop | M12 A-coded (dedicated) | IP67 | 4 A | IEC 60947-5-5 | Safety relay circuits |
| High-power (>25A) | M23 / M40 Power | IP67 | 28–75 A | IEC 61076-2-113 | Heavy payload robot |
| Pneumatic | Push-in fittings (8mm) | IP67 | N/A | ISO 14743 | Gripper actuation |
| Tool changer | Heavy-duty rectangular | IP65 | 30+ A | IEC 60603-1 | End-effector swap |
| Battery (mobile robot) | High-current locking | IP54 | 100+ A | UL 1977 | AGV/AMR charging |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define an electronic connector as per IEC 61984 and list any four key electrical performance parameters used to specify a connector.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IEC 61984:2008**, an electronic connector is defined as *"a component which terminates conductors for the purpose of providing connection and disconnection to a suitable mating component."*

The four key electrical performance parameters are:
1. **Rated Voltage ($U_r$):** The maximum voltage the connector can continuously withstand.
2. **Rated Current ($I_r$):** The maximum continuous current the connector can carry without exceeding its temperature limit.
3. **Contact Resistance ($R_c$):** The electrical resistance of the mated contact pair, typically in milliohms.
4. **Insulation Resistance ($R_i$):** The resistance between adjacent contacts or between contacts and the housing, typically greater than 1000 MΩ.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is an IP rating? Explain the meaning of IP67 with reference to IEC 60529.**

**Model Answer:**

**IP (Ingress Protection) rating**, as defined by **IEC 60529**, is a two-digit code that classifies the degree of protection provided by an enclosure (such as a connector housing) against the intrusion of solid foreign objects and liquids.

**IP67 breakdown:**
- **First digit "6":** **Dust-tight** — Complete protection against the ingress of solid particles, including dust. The highest level of solid particle protection.
- **Second digit "7":** **Immersion protection** — Protection against temporary immersion in water up to 1 meter depth for 30 minutes.

In robotic applications, **IP67-rated connectors** (such as M12 connectors) are commonly used for sensors and actuators on industrial robots that may be exposed to coolant spray, washdown cleaning, or outdoor environments.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the constructional features of a typical industrial M12 circular connector. Discuss the role of each component: housing, insulator, contacts, plating, and sealing elements. How do these features contribute to IP67 protection?

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A robot controller uses a power connector rated at 25 A at 40 °C. The maximum operating temperature is 125 °C. If the robot operates in an ambient temperature of 75 °C, calculate the derated current using the standard derating formula. What is the percentage reduction in current carrying capacity?

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The **M12 circular connector** is the most widely used connector family in industrial robotics. Its constructional features include:

**1. Housing (Outer Shell):**
- Material: Nickel-plated brass, stainless steel, or high-grade thermoplastic (PBT, PA66)
- Function: Provides mechanical strength, environmental protection, and EMI/RFI shielding
- Thread type: M12 × 1 mm pitch for secure coupling

**2. Insulator (Insert/Dielectric):**
- Material: Thermoplastic such as PA (Polyamide) or PBT (Polybutylene Terephthalate)
- Function: Electrically isolates individual contacts and provides mechanical retention
- Color coding (A, B, D, X, K, L, S, T) indicates the keying and pin arrangement

**3. Contacts:**
- Material: Phosphor bronze or beryllium copper (spring properties)
- Plating: Gold (0.2–2.5 μm) over nickel (2–5 μm)
- Function: Provides the actual electrical conduction path with spring-loaded normal force

**4. Sealing Elements:**
- O-rings: NBR (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber) or FKM (Viton) at the coupling interface
- Moulded seals: At the cable entry and contact area
- Function: Provide IP67 protection by preventing dust and water ingress

**5. Locking Mechanism:**
- Threaded coupling nut (standard) or push-pull locking (for high-vibration applications)
- Function: Maintains normal force and prevents accidental disconnection

**Contribution to IP67 Protection:**
- The **O-ring seal** between the male and female connector bodies prevents water ingress during coupling (addressing digit "7").
- The **moulded cable seal** prevents water entry through the cable entry point.
- The **tight tolerance** of the housing threads creates a dust-tight barrier (addressing digit "6").

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
$$I_{rated} = 25 \text{ A}, \quad T_{rated} = 40°\text{C}, \quad T_{max} = 125°\text{C}, \quad T_{ambient} = 75°\text{C}$$

**Step 1: Apply the derating formula**

$$I_{derated} = I_{rated} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T_{max} - T_{ambient}}{T_{max} - T_{rated}}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$I_{derated} = 25 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{125 - 75}{125 - 40}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the temperature differentials**

$$I_{derated} = 25 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{50}{85}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the ratio**

$$\frac{50}{85} = 0.5882$$

**Step 5: Take the square root**

$$\sqrt{0.5882} = 0.7670$$

**Step 6: Calculate the derated current**

$$I_{derated} = 25 \cdot 0.7670 = 19.18 \text{ A}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the percentage reduction**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{25 - 19.18}{25} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{5.82}{25} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Reduction} = 23.28\%}$$

The connector can only carry **19.18 A** in this high-temperature environment, representing a **23.28% reduction** from its nominal rating.
