### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With neat sketches, classify the different types of electronic connectors used in robotic systems. Discuss the application of each type with reference to at least one relevant IEC standard.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A gold-plated connector contact has the following specifications: contact area radius = 30 μm, number of contact spots = 4, copper base material with resistivity = 1.68 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m, bulk resistance = 0.3 mΩ, and film resistance = 0.05 mΩ. Calculate the total contact resistance and the voltage drop when a current of 5 A flows through the connector.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Classification of Connectors Used in Robotics:**

**1. By Function:**
- **Power Connectors (IEC 61076-2-111):** M12 K-coded (AC) and L-coded (DC) connectors. Used for supplying power to servo motors and drives.
- **Signal Connectors (IEC 61076-2-101):** M12 A-coded connectors. Used for sensor signals and encoder feedback.
- **Data Connectors (IEC 61076-2-109):** M12 X-coded connectors for 10 Gigabit Ethernet. Used for vision systems and high-speed communication.
- **Coaxial/RF Connectors (IEC 61169):** Used for radar and wireless communication in mobile robots.
- **Fiber Optic Connectors (IEC 61754):** Used in long-distance, EMI-immune communication links in large robotic cells.

**2. By Mounting:**
- **Panel Mount:** Fixed to the robot body or control cabinet wall.
- **PCB Mount:** Soldered onto the controller's printed circuit board.
- **Cable Mount:** Attached to the cable end (most common in robotic harnesses).
- **In-line Splice:** Used for joining two cables in long cable runs.

**3. By Environmental Protection:**
- **IP20 (IEC 60529):** Indoor cabinet connectors (no protection).
- **IP67:** Sealed connectors for washdown environments (food industry robots).
- **IP69K:** High-pressure, high-temperature washdown (automotive painting robots).

**Application Examples with Standards:**

| **Connector Type** | **IEC Standard** | **Application in Robotics** |
|-------------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| M12 A-coded 4-pin | IEC 61076-2-101 | Encoder feedback from joint motors |
| M12 D-coded 4-pin | IEC 61076-2-109 | PROFINET/EtherCAT communication |
| M12 X-coded 8-pin | IEC 61076-2-109 | Vision system data (10 Gbit Ethernet) |
| M12 K-coded 5-pin | IEC 61076-2-111 | AC power to servo motor (up to 16 A) |
| M23 19-pin | IEC 61076-2-114 | Heavy-duty signal harness |
| Han Industrial | IEC 60603-1 | Modular power/signal at tool changer |

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Contact spot radius: $a = 30$ μm $= 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Number of contact spots: $n = 4$
- Resistivity: $\rho = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m
- Bulk resistance: $R_{bulk} = 0.3$ mΩ
- Film resistance: $R_{film} = 0.05$ mΩ
- Current: $I = 5$ A

**Step 1: Calculate the constriction resistance for a single a-spot**

$$R_{constriction,single} = \frac{\rho}{2a}$$

$$R_{constriction,single} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{constriction,single} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-8}}{60 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{constriction,single} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Ω} = 0.28 \text{ mΩ}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total constriction resistance for 4 parallel spots**

$$R_{constriction,total} = \frac{R_{constriction,single}}{n} = \frac{0.28}{4} = 0.07 \text{ mΩ}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total contact resistance**

$$R_c = R_{bulk} + R_{constriction,total} + R_{film}$$

$$R_c = 0.3 + 0.07 + 0.05$$

$$\boxed{R_c = 0.42 \text{ mΩ}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the voltage drop across the connector**

$$V_{drop} = I \cdot R_c$$

$$V_{drop} = 5 \cdot 0.42 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{V_{drop} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.1 \text{ mV}}$$

This very low voltage drop (**2.1 mV**) demonstrates why gold-plated connectors are preferred in robotic systems where signal integrity is critical for encoder feedback and data communication.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Always convert μm to m (×10⁻⁶) when using SI units in contact resistance calculations. Many students lose 2–3 marks by leaving inconsistent units.
> 2. **Confusing creepage and clearance** — Creepage is measured **along the insulator surface**; clearance is measured **through air**. Both are different and both matter for IEC 60664-1 compliance.
> 3. **Wrong derating formula application** — The derating formula uses **temperature differentials** ($T_{max} - T_{ambient}$) and ($T_{max} - T_{rated}$), NOT the absolute temperatures themselves.
> 4. **Neglecting parallel contact spots** — Real connectors have multiple microscopic a-spots in parallel; students often calculate constriction resistance for one spot and forget to divide by $n$.
> 5. **Confusing IP rating digits** — The first digit is **solids** (0–6), the second is **liquids** (0–9K). IPX7 is NOT the same as IP6X.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Connectors for Electronic Equipment:**

- ✅ **IEC 61984** is the primary safety standard for connectors in industrial applications.
- ✅ **Contact resistance** has three components: $R_{bulk} + R_{constriction} + R_{film}$.
- ✅ **Constriction resistance** is governed by Holm's equation: $R_{constriction} = \rho / (2a)$.
- ✅ The **100-gram rule** states that a minimum normal force of 0.98 N is required for reliable contact.
- ✅ **Gold plating** (typically 0.2–2.5 μm) over nickel provides low resistance and corrosion protection.
- ✅ **IP rating format is IPXY** — first digit = solids (0–6), second digit = liquids (0–9K).
- ✅ **M12 connectors** are the industry standard for robotic applications, with coding variants: A (signal), D (Ethernet), X (10GbE), K (AC power), L (DC power).
- ✅ **Current derating** follows the square-root law based on temperature differential.
- ✅ **Creepage distance** is given by $d_{creepage} \geq k \cdot U_r^{0.8}$ per IEC 60664-1.
- ✅ **Insertion force** $F_{ins} = \mu \cdot n \cdot F_n$ — depends on friction coefficient, number of contacts, and normal force.
- ✅ **Safety-critical connectors** in E-stop circuits require **PL e / SIL 3** rating per ISO 13849-1 and IEC 62061.
- ✅ **Mating cycles** indicate service life — typical industrial connectors: 100 to 500 cycles; high-end: up to 10,000 cycles.
- ✅ **Shielded connectors** with metal housings provide **EMI/RFI immunity** essential for EtherCAT and PROFINET communication.
- ✅ **Operating temperature** range for industrial connectors is typically **-25 °C to +85 °C**; extended range: **-55 °C to +200 °C**.
- ✅ **Locking mechanisms** include threaded coupling (standard), push-pull (high-vibration), and bayonet (quick-mate).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety of Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT417 - ROBOT SAFETY AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety of Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety of Machinery** is the discipline of engineering that applies a systematic, risk-based methodology — formalized internationally by **ISO 12100:2010** *“Safety of machinery — General principles for design — Risk assessment and risk reduction”* and within the European Union by the **Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC** — to ensure that machinery, including industrial robots and their integrated workcells, is designed, constructed, and maintained so that hazards to persons (operators, programmers, maintenance staff, bystanders) are either eliminated or reduced to an acceptable residual risk throughout the machine's intended lifecycle.

The term *acceptable risk* is **not zero risk**; it is the risk level the manufacturer and end-user jointly agree upon after applying the **three-step iterative methodology** prescribed by ISO 12100:

1. **Inherent safe design measures** (eliminate hazards at source)
2. **Safeguarding and complementary protective measures** (guards, interlocks, sensors)
3. **Information for use** (signage, manuals, training, PPE)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Every answer you write on this topic MUST be anchored to the *ISO 12100 risk-reduction triangle* and the *Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC* legal framework. Examiners explicitly award marks for citing the correct standard clause (e.g., EN ISO 13849-1, EN 60204-1).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are designing a new home **mixer-grinder**. Before selling it, a safety engineer asks five questions:

| Real-World Question | Engineering Translation |
|---|---|
| Can we redesign the blade so the finger can never reach it? | **Inherent safe design** |
| If the lid is open, can we prevent the motor from starting? | **Interlocking safeguard** |
| Can we add a sensor that stops the blade in 50 ms if a hand is detected? | **Protective device** |
| What does the warning sticker say? | **Information for use** |
| How dangerous is it if all the above fail? | **Residual risk** |

This is the **exact same logic** used for a 6-axis industrial robot cell weighing **1,500 kg**, except the engineering standards become mandatory (ISO 10218-1, ISO 10218-2, ISO/TS 15066) and the failure of a safeguard can be **fatal**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants, Metrics & Standard Terms (in **bold**)

- **Performance Level (PL):** Discrete level (a, b, c, d, e) specifying the reliability of safety-related control system parts (per **EN ISO 13849-1**). **PL e** = highest reliability; **PL a** = lowest.
- **Category (Cat):** Structural architecture classification (**B, 1, 2, 3, 4**) of safety-related parts.
- **Mean Time To Dangerous Failure (MTTFd):** Expected lifetime before a dangerous failure, measured in **years**.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** Percentage of dangerous failures detected by automatic diagnostics, expressed as a percentage (**%**).
- **Common Cause Failure (CCF):** Failure of multiple components due to a single shared cause.
- **Dangerous Failure (DF):** Failure that prevents the safety function from operating.
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):** Ratio of safe + diagnosed dangerous failures to total failures, expressed in **%**.
- **Stop Categories** (per **EN 60204-1**): **Stop Category 0** (uncontrolled, immediate power removal), **Stop Category 1** (controlled stop with power removal after stop), **Stop Category 2** (controlled stop with power retained).
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Discrete level (**1, 2, 3, 4**) per **IEC 61508**, used primarily in process industries but mathematically mappable to PL.
- **Risk:** Combination of **severity of harm** and **probability of occurrence** of that harm.

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchical relationship is: **Severity + Frequency + Possibility of Avoidance + Probability of Occurrence → Risk Graph → Required PL → Required Category**.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISO 12100 Risk-Reduction Methodology (Structured)

The entire machinery safety discipline rests on the following **iterative loop**:

### Step 1 — Determination of Limits (Clause 5.1)
- **Use limits:** intended purpose, operating modes, user skill level.
- **Space limits:** range of motion, mounting, energy supply.
- **Time limits:** service life, maintenance intervals.
- **Environmental limits:** temperature, humidity, EMC, vibration.

### Step 2 — Hazard Identification (Clause 5.2)
- **Mechanical:** crushing, shearing, cutting, entanglement, drawing-in, stabbing, impact, crushing, high-pressure fluid injection.
- **Electrical:** shock, burn, arc flash, electrostatic discharge.
- **Thermal:** burns, fire.
- **Noise:** hearing loss, distraction.
- **Vibration:** HAVS (Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome), WBV (Whole-Body Vibration).
- **Radiation:** ionising, non-ionising, laser.
- **Chemical / Biological:** dust, fumes, biological agents.
- **Ergonomic:** repetitive strain, lifting, posture, mental overload.
- **Slip / Trip / Fall.**
- **Combinations of the above.**

### Step 3 — Risk Estimation (Clause 5.3)
Each hazard is scored on the four parameters of the **risk graph** (see Section 3 for derivation):

| Parameter | Levels | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **S** — Severity of injury | S1, S2 | Slight / Serious (irreversible, death) |
| **F** — Frequency/Exposure | F1, F2 | Rare-to-often / Frequent-to-continuous |
| **P** — Possibility of avoidance | P1, P2 | Possible under specific conditions / Almost impossible |
| **O** — Probability of hazardous event | O1, O2 | Low / High |

### Step 4 — Risk Evaluation
Compare the estimated risk against the **benefit / state of the art / legal requirements**. If unacceptable → continue.

### Step 5 — Risk Reduction Measures (the **3-step hierarchy**, Clause 6)
1. **Inherent safe design:** removal of hazard by changing the machine itself (e.g., smaller power, lower speed, safer geometry, eliminating stored energy).
2. **Safeguarding:** guards (fixed, interlocked, movable, adjustable), protective devices (two-hand controls, light curtains, safety mats, laser scanners, emergency stop), complementary measures.
3. **Information for use:** warnings, signs, procedures, training, PPE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **order is mandatory and non-negotiable**. You *cannot* put a warning sticker on a blade and call the machine safe — you must first try to eliminate the hazard.

## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Standards (KTU High-Yield)

European safety standards are organised into **three tiers**:

| Tier | Type | Scope | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Basic safety standards | General, applicable to **all** machinery | **EN ISO 12100** (risk assessment), EN ISO 14159 (hygiene) |
| **B1** | Generic safety standards | Specific aspect, multiple machine types | **EN ISO 13849-1** (control systems safety), EN ISO 13850 (emergency stop), EN 60204-1 (electrical equipment), EN ISO 13857 (safety distances) |
| **B2** | Generic safety standards | Specific aspect, single machine type | EN ISO 13851 (two-hand control), EN ISO 13856 (pressure-sensitive devices) |
| **C** | Machine-specific safety standards | Detailed safety requirements for **one** machine type | **EN ISO 10218-1/2** (industrial robots), **ISO/TS 15066** (collaborative robots), EN 474 (earth-moving machinery) |

> When a Type-C standard exists, it **takes precedence** over Type-A and Type-B standards. Where a Type-C standard is silent, the Type-B then Type-A standards apply.

## 2.3 Safety Categories (EN ISO 13849-1)

| Category | Behaviour on Single Fault | Use of Proven Components | Diagnostic Coverage | Typical MTTFd | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B** | No special requirements | No | None | Low | Basic relay logic |
| **1** | No special requirements | **Yes (well-tried)** | None | High | Single contactor with mirror contacts |
| **2** | Single fault **may** lead to loss of safety function, but **is detected** | Yes | **DC low** (≥ 60%) | Medium to high | Redundant sensors with periodic test |
| **3** | Single fault **does not** lead to loss of safety function (single fault tolerance) | Yes | None required | Medium to high | Dual-channel with monitoring |
| **4** | Single fault **does not** lead to loss of safety function; fault **accumulation is detected** | Yes | High (DC ≥ 99%) | High | Dual-channel with cross-monitoring + diagnostics |

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Risk} = f(S, F, P, O)$ | Risk is a function of the four graph parameters | dimensionless index |
| 2 | $\text{PL} = f(\text{Category}, \text{DC}, \text{MTTFd}, \text{CCF})$ | Performance Level depends on architecture + reliability | PL a–e |
| 3 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{safe}} + \lambda_{\text{dd}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Safe Failure Fraction | **%** |
| 4 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{dd}}}{\lambda_{\text{dd}} + \lambda_{\text{du}}} \times 100\%$ | Diagnostic Coverage | **%** |
| 5 | $\text{PFH}_d$ | Average probability of dangerous failure per hour (used in SIL/PL verification) | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\text{MTTFd} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_d}$ | Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | **years** |
| 7 | $\text{PL} \leftrightarrow \text{SIL}$ mapping (high-level): PL a ↔ SIL 1, PL b/c ↔ SIL 1, PL d ↔ SIL 2, PL e ↔ SIL 3 | Approximate cross-mapping (informative Annex) | — |
| 8 | Safety distance (perpendicular approach, ISO 13857): $S = (K \times T) + C$ | Minimum separation to a hazard | **mm** |
| 9 | $K$ | Approach speed: hand **2,000 mm/s**, body **1,600 mm/s** | mm/s |
| 10 | $T$ | Total stopping time (machine + safety device reaction) | **s** |
| 11 | $C$ | Intrusion distance into the sensing field before triggering | **mm** |
| 12 | $\text{PF} = \text{PL}^a$ (Bar chart, Annex E of EN ISO 13849-1) | Probability of dangerous failure per hour read from graph | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The table above deliberately uses `\dfrac` and `\times` instead of the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol so that the markdown table renders correctly. If you are writing this in LaTeX, you may freely substitute `\vert` for absolute value notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Framework

- **Robot integrators** must conduct a CE-marking conformity assessment and produce a **Technical File** (Annex I of 2006/42/EC).
- **Plant safety officers** use the same ISO 12100 logic to perform **workplace risk assessments** as required by India's **Factories Act 1948** and the **OSH Code 2020**.
- **Functional safety engineers** map the required **PL** to specific **PFHd** budgets when designing safety PLCs (Pilz PNOZ, Siemens F-CPU, Allen-Bradley GuardLogix).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safety Distance (ISO 13855 / ISO 13857)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to derive and apply the safety distance formula. Let us derive it from first principles.

> **Problem setup.** A horizontal light curtain (resolution ≤ 14 mm, finger detection) guards a robot cell. The operator stands upright and approaches perpendicular to the sensing field. The light curtain is **500 mm** away from the nearest hazard point. The machine's stopping performance is measured as follows:
> - Reaction time of the light curtain (test rod detection → output OFF): $t_1 = 30 \text{ ms}$
> - Reaction time of the safety relay / controller: $t_2 = 15 \text{ ms}$
> - Mechanical / hydraulic stopping time of the robot: $t_3 = 250 \text{ ms}$
> - Additional worst-case time for any safety module: $t_4 = 10 \text{ ms}$
> - Intrusion distance (C) for a finger-detection device: $C = 8 \times (d - 14)$ but not less than 0, where $d$ = resolution. Given $d = 14$ mm → $C = 0$ mm.
> - Approach speed $K = 2{,}000$ mm/s (hand approach, ISO 13855 Table 1).

**Step 1.** Sum the reaction times to obtain the total stop time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 \\
  &= 30 \text{ ms} + 15 \text{ ms} + 250 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms} \\
  &= 305 \text{ ms} \\
  &= 0.305 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The total time $T$ is the interval between the moment a body part enters the sensing field and the moment the hazardous motion has ceased. We must include **every** delay because the worst case determines safety.

**Step 2.** Compute the distance travelled by the hand during the stop time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{travel}} &= K \times T \\
                  &= 2{,}000 \, \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}} \times 0.305 \, \text{s} \\
                  &= 610 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* During $T$, the hand keeps moving toward the hazard at speed $K$. We must ensure it cannot physically reach the hazard before the machine stops.

**Step 3.** Compute the safety distance using the standard ISO 13855 formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (K \times T) + C \\
  &= 610 \, \text{mm} + 0 \, \text{mm} \\
  &= 610 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $S$ is the **minimum** distance from the hazard to the nearest edge of the sensing field.

**Step 4.** Check the installation:

The light curtain is currently installed at **500 mm** from the hazard.

$$
S_{\text{installed}} = 500 \, \text{mm} \; < \; S_{\text{required}} = 610 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **NON-COMPLIANT INSTALLATION.** The integrator must move the light curtain **at least 110 mm farther** from the hazard, or reduce the stop time (faster robot brakes, faster relay) to lower $T$ to at most 245 ms.

**Step 5.** Required minimum installation distance from the hazard:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\min} &= (K \times T) + C \\
        &= (2{,}000 \times 0.245) + 0 \\
        &= 490 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Any installation distance $\geq 490$ mm keeps the existing light curtain within compliance **only if** $T$ is reduced to 245 ms — for example, by upgrading the safety relay to a model with $t_2 = 0$ ms (e.g., solid-state safety output).

## 3.2 Derivation: Diagnostic Coverage from Failure Rates

> **Given.** A safety switch (Category 3, dual-channel) has the following failure rates:
> - $\lambda_{\text{dd}} = 0.3 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{h}^{-1}$ (dangerous failures, detected by diagnostics)
> - $\lambda_{\text{du}} = 0.7 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{h}^{-1}$ (dangerous failures, undetected)
> - $\lambda_{\text{safe}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{h}^{-1}$

**Step 1.** Compute the Diagnostic Coverage using the standard EN ISO 13849-1 formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC} &= \frac{\lambda_{\text{dd}}}{\lambda_{\text{dd}} + \lambda_{\text{du}}} \times 100\% \\
         &= \frac{0.3}{0.3 + 0.7} \times 100\% \\
         &= 0.3 \times 100\% \\
         &= 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Only 30% of dangerous failures are caught by the self-diagnostic routine. This is **below** the EN ISO 13849-1 minimum of **60%** for Category 2 systems. The system would **not** meet the required DC for any safety function above PL a.

**Step 2.** Compute the Safe Failure Fraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\text{total}} &= \lambda_{\text{safe}} + \lambda_{\text{dd}} + \lambda_{\text{du}} \\
                       &= 1.0 + 0.3 + 0.7 \\
                       &= 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SFF} &= \frac{\lambda_{\text{safe}} + \lambda_{\text{dd}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\
          &= \frac{1.0 + 0.3}{2.0} \times 100\% \\
          &= 65\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* SFF = 65%. For Category 3 / PL d, EN ISO 13849-1 requires SFF ≥ 60% (tolerable). The system **passes** the SFF check but **fails** the DC check.

**Step 3.** Engineering recommendation: add **cross-channel monitoring** with periodic test pulses to detect the 70% of failures currently slipping through. With improved diagnostics, the achievable DC could rise to **≥ 90%** (DCavg = high), which would then support PL d or PL e depending on the architecture.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Safeguarding Technology

| Hazard | First choice (Inherent) | First choice (Safeguard) | Complementary |
|---|---|---|---|
| Robot motion (fencing) | Reduce reach, use speed/force limits | Fixed perimeter guard (ISO 13857) | Safety door interlock (Cat 3+) |
| Robot motion (collaborative) | Power & Force Limiting (PFL) design | Hand-guiding device + speed monitor | ISO/TS 15066 bio-mechanical limits |
| Press (mechanical) | Redesign tool to eliminate pinch | Light curtain (Type 3/4) | Two-hand control (ISO 13851) |
| Conveyor (draw-in) | Eliminate nip point geometry | Fixed guard with viewing panel | Emergency pull-cord (ISO 13850) |
| Fume / chemical | Substitute non-hazardous chemical | Local exhaust ventilation | PPE (respirator), MSDS, training |
| Electrical | SELV/PELV design | Insulation, IP2X enclosure | Lockout/Tagout (LOTO), sign |
| Hydraulic injection | Reduce pressure to < 50 bar | Hose burst valves, whip-check | Maintenance procedure |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 12100 Risk-Reduction Iterative Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["step1 Determine Machine Limits"] --> B["step2 Hazard Identification"]
    B --> C["step3 Risk Estimation S F P O"]
    C --> D{"step4 Risk Acceptable?"}
    D -- "No" --> E["step5a Inherent Safe Design"]
    E --> F["step5b Safeguarding Guards and Devices"]
    F --> G["step5c Information for Use Signs and Training"]
    G --> C
    D -- "Yes" --> H["step6 Document in Technical File"]
    H --> I["step7 CE Marking and Declaration of Conformity"]
```

*Logic:* The loop is **iterative** — every protective measure is re-checked against residual risk. Documentation is the **final**, non-optional step.

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Standards (Type A / B1 / B2 / C)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TYPEC["Type C Machine Specific Standard e.g. EN ISO 10218"]
    TYPEC --> TYPB["Type B Generic Safety Standard B1 General e.g. EN ISO 13849 B2 Specific e.g. EN ISO 13851"]
    TYPB --> TYPA["Type A Basic Safety Standard e.g. EN ISO 12100"]
    TYPA --> EUREG["EU Regulation Machinery Directive 2006 42 EC"]
    EUREG --> OSH["Indian National OSH Code 2020 Factories Act 1948"]
```

*Logic:* Lower-numbered tiers override higher tiers (Type C wins over Type B wins over Type A).

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Two-Channel Safety System (Category 3, PL d)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["Input Channel 1 E Stop Pushbutton 1"] --> LOG1["Safety Logic Channel 1 Contactor 1"]
    IN2["Input Channel 2 E Stop Pushbutton 2"] --> LOG2["Safety Logic Channel 2 Contactor 2"]
    LOG1 --> MON["Cross Monitor Diagnostic Module"]
    LOG2 --> MON
    MON --> OUT["Output to Robot Drive Enable Signal"]
    MON -- "Fault Detected" --> FAULT["Fault Latch and Indicator"]
```

*Logic:* Two independent channels, each capable of removing the safety-relevant output on its own. The cross-monitor detects single faults and prevents re-start. Together this architecture **achieves Category 3 / PL d** provided MTTFd of each channel is High and DCavg is Low or better.

## 4.4 Risk Graph (ISO 12100, EN ISO 13849-1 Annex A)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["S1 Slight Injury"] --> P1["P1 Avoidable"]
    S1 --> P2["P2 Hard to Avoid"]
    S1 --> Q1["O1 Low Probability of Hazardous Event"]
    S1 --> Q2["O2 High Probability of Hazardous Event"]
    S2["S2 Serious Injury or Death"] --> R1["P1 Avoidable"]
    S2 --> R2["P2 Hard to Avoid"]
    Q1 --> F1["F1 Rare to Sometimes"]
    Q1 --> F2["F2 Frequent to Continuous"]
    Q2 --> F2
    F1 --> PL1["Required PL a or b"]
    F2 --> PL2["Required PL c or d"]
    R2 --> PL3["Required PL d or e"]
    R1 --> PL2
    P1 --> PL2
    P2 --> PL3
```

*Logic:* Reading the risk graph: start at severity, follow the branch through frequency of exposure, then possibility of avoidance, then probability of occurrence. The endpoint is the **required Performance Level (PLr)**.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Robot Cell Safety System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAYER1["Layer 1 Physical Safeguards"]
        G1["Fixed Perimeter Fence 1.8 m high"]
        G2["Safety Light Curtain Type 4"]
        G3["Safety Mat Pressure Sensitive"]
        G4["Emergency Stop Pushbuttons 3 locations"]
    end
    subgraph LAYER2["Layer 2 Interlocking and Control"]
        S1["Safety Door Interlock Cat 4 PL e"]
        S2["Safety PLC Pilz PNOZmulti 2"]
        S3["Robot Safety Controller integrated"]
    end
    subgraph LAYER3["Layer 3 Robot and Drive"]
        R1["Robot Drive Stop Cat 1 SS1"]
        R2["Safe Torque Off STO"]
        R3["Safely Limited Speed SLS"]
    end
    subgraph LAYER4["Layer 4 Information and Documentation"]
        D1["Risk Assessment File"]
        D2["Operating Manual"]
        D3["Training Records"]
        D4["CE Declaration of Conformity"]
    end
    LAYER1 --> LAYER2
    LAYER2 --> LAYER3
    LAYER3 --> LAYER4
    LAYER4 -.Feedback Loop.-> LAYER1
```

*Logic:* The four layers operate as an **integrated whole**; documentation feeds back into a periodic review (typically annually) which may re-trigger changes to physical safeguards. This is the **functional architecture** that every robot cell integrator must design and present to the KTU examiner.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Safety of Machinery** as per **ISO 12100:2010**. List the **three iterative steps** used for risk reduction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** *Safety of machinery* is the application of a systematic, risk-based design methodology — formalised in ISO 12100:2010 — to ensure that hazards are eliminated or reduced to an acceptable residual risk throughout the machine's intended use.
>
> **Three iterative steps of risk reduction (2 marks — 1 mark each step, both steps must be named correctly):**
> 1. **Inherent safe design** measures (eliminate the hazard at source, e.g., reduce power, change geometry).
> 2. **Safeguarding and complementary protective measures** (guards, interlocks, light curtains, emergency stop).
> 3. **Information for use** (warnings, signs, manuals, training, personal protective equipment).
>
> The loop is **iterative**: after every measure, the residual risk is re-estimated; if still unacceptable, the cycle is repeated starting again from step 1.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **Type A, Type B, and Type C** safety standards with **one example** for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Standard Type | Scope (1 mark) | Example (½ mark) | Example (½ mark) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type A — Basic Safety Standard** | General, applicable to **all** machinery | **EN ISO 12100** | — |
| **Type B — Generic Safety Standard** | Specific safety aspect, multiple machine types; subdivided into **B1** (general) and **B2** (specific) | **EN ISO 13849-1** (B1) | **EN ISO 13851** (B2, two-hand control) |
| **Type C — Machine-Specific Safety Standard** | Detailed safety requirements for **one** machine type | **EN ISO 10218-1/2** (industrial robots) | **ISO/TS 15066** (collaborative robots) |

> **Precedence rule (½ mark):** When a Type-C standard exists, it overrides Type-B and Type-A. Where silent, the higher tier applies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A and Question B are entirely independent. Answer EITHER A OR B in full.**

---

### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **ISO 12100 risk-reduction methodology** in detail. Clearly state the **three-step hierarchy of risk reduction** and the role of **risk estimation parameters (S, F, P, O)**.
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A horizontal **safety light curtain** (finger detection, resolution $d = 14$ mm) protects a hazard point. The total stop time of the machine is $T = 0.4$ s. Calculate the **minimum safety distance** $S$ from the hazard to the sensing field. State the **installation compliance** if the light curtain is currently placed **700 mm** from the hazard. Use $K = 2{,}000$ mm/s, $C = 0$ mm (for resolution $\leq 14$ mm).
>
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) [7 marks]

> **Diagram (2 marks):** The full ISO 12100 iterative loop (as drawn in Section 4.1 above). Must show the loop from *Determine Limits* → *Hazard Identification* → *Risk Estimation* → *Risk Evaluation* → *Risk Reduction* → back to *Risk Estimation*.

> **Three-step hierarchy of risk reduction (3 marks — 1 each):**
> 1. **Inherent safe design** — change the machine itself. Examples: reduce power, lower voltage, substitute chemicals, limit range of motion, design out sharp edges.
> 2. **Safeguarding and complementary protective measures** — add guards, interlocks, two-hand controls, light curtains, emergency stop, pressure-sensitive mats.
> 3. **Information for use** — warnings, signs, operating instructions, training, PPE.

> **Risk estimation parameters (2 marks — ½ each):**
> - **S — Severity of injury** (S1 slight / S2 serious, irreversible, death)
> - **F — Frequency / duration of exposure** (F1 rare-to-sometimes / F2 frequent-to-continuous)
> - **P — Possibility of avoidance** (P1 possible under specific conditions / P2 almost impossible)
> - **O — Probability of occurrence of hazardous event** (O1 low / O2 high)

> These four parameters together yield the **required Performance Level (PLr)** on the risk graph.

#### Model Answer — (b) [7 marks]

> **Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
> $K = 2{,}000$ mm/s; $T = 0.4$ s; $C = 0$ mm (since $d \le 14$ mm).

> **Step 2 — Apply the safety distance formula (2 marks):**

$$
S = (K \times T) + C
$$

> **Step 3 — Substitute and compute (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (2{,}000 \, \text{mm/s} \times 0.4 \, \text{s}) + 0 \, \text{mm} \\
  &= 800 \, \text{mm} + 0 \, \text{mm} \\
  &= 800 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4 — Compare with installed distance (1 mark):**
> $S_{\text{installed}} = 700$ mm $<$ $S_{\text{required}} = 800$ mm.

> **Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The installation is **NON-COMPLIANT** with ISO 13855. The light curtain must be moved outward to at least **800 mm** from the hazard (i.e., relocated 100 mm farther away) or the stop time must be reduced to $\le 0.35$ s to restore compliance.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Categories of safety-related control system parts** (B, 1, 2, 3, 4) as per **EN ISO 13849-1**, with a comparison table. State the **relationship between Category, MTTFd, DC, and resulting Performance Level (PL)**.
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Category 3 dual-channel safety relay has the following parameters:
> - Channel 1: $\text{MTTFd}_1 = 60$ years
> - Channel 2: $\text{MTTFd}_2 = 80$ years
> - Diagnostic Coverage: $\text{DC}_{\text{avg}} = 90\%$
> - CCF measures: implemented and scored 65/100.
>
> Determine the **achievable Performance Level (PL)** for the safety function using the **bar-chart method of EN ISO 13849-1 Annex E**. Justify the answer.
>
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) [7 marks]

> **Introduction (1 mark):** EN ISO 13849-1 defines five Categories (B, 1, 2, 3, 4) describing the **structural architecture** of safety-related parts of control systems (SRP/CS), and combines them with reliability parameters (MTTFd, DC, CCF) to derive a **Performance Level (PL a to e)**.

> **Categories comparison table (3 marks):**

| Category | Behaviour on a Single Fault | Use of Well-Tried Components | Diagnostic Coverage | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B** | No special requirement | No | None | Basic relay logic |
| **1** | No special requirement | **Yes** | None | Single contactor with mirror contact |
| **2** | Single fault may lead to loss of safety function, **but is detected** | Yes | **Low ≥ 60%** | Redundant sensors + periodic test pulse |
| **3** | Single fault **does not** lead to loss of safety function (single-fault tolerance) | Yes | None required | Dual-channel with cross-monitoring |
| **4** | Single fault does not lead to loss; **fault accumulation is detected** | Yes | **High ≥ 99%** | Dual-channel with full diagnostics |

> **Relationship — Category + MTTFd + DC → PL (3 marks — 1 each for the four corners):**
> - **PL a:** Category B, low MTTFd, DC = none.
> - **PL b/c:** Category 1, medium MTTFd, DC = none.
> - **PL c/d:** Category 2 with DCavg = low to medium, MTTFd = medium to high.
> - **PL d/e:** Category 3 with MTTFd = high and DCavg = low to medium (for PL d), OR Category 4 with MTTFd = high and DCavg = high (for PL e).
>
> The **bar chart (Annex E)** cross-references the four parameters and outputs a single PL value from **a to e**.

#### Model Answer — (b) [7 marks]

> **Step 1 — Compute the combined MTTFd for the two channels (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\text{MTTFd}_{\text{channel}}} &= \frac{1}{60} + \frac{1}{80} \\
                                     &= \frac{4 + 3}{240} \\
                                     &= \frac{7}{240} \\
\text{MTTFd}_{\text{channel}} &= \frac{240}{7} \approx 34.3 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 2 — Classify MTTFd band (1 mark):**
> Per EN ISO 13849-1 Table 5:
> - MTTFd < 3 years → Low
> - 3 ≤ MTTFd < 10 years → Medium
> - 10 ≤ MTTFd < 30 years → High
> - 30 ≤ MTTFd ≤ 100 years → High
>
> $34.3$ years → **High** band.

> **Step 3 — Classify DC band (1 mark):**
> - DC < 60% → None
> - 60% ≤ DC < 90% → Low
> - 90% ≤ DC ≤ 99% → Medium
> - DC = 99% → High
>
> $\text{DC}_{\text{avg}} = 90\%$ → boundary case, **Low → Medium** (use exact 90% as "Low" in the chart per common practice, or accept 90% as Medium in some interpretations).

> **Step 4 — Evaluate CCF (1 mark):**
> Score 65/100 ≥ 65 → **CCF requirements satisfied** (minimum 65 per ISO 13849-1 Annex F).

> **Step 5 — Read PL from the bar chart (2 marks):**
> - Category = 3
> - MTTFd band = High
> - DCavg = Low (90% threshold) or Medium
> - CCF = satisfied
>
> **Result:** With Category 3, MTTFd = High, DCavg = Low, the bar chart yields **PL d**. (If DCavg is interpreted as Medium, the result is still **PL d**; PL e would require Category 4.)

> **Conclusion:** The safety function **achieves PL d**, which is the typical level required for **robot cell perimeter interlocks and emergency stop circuits** under EN ISO 10218-1.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in "Safety of Machinery" questions:**
> 1. **Citing only "ISO 12100" without the year 2010** — Examiners expect **ISO 12100:2010** (½ to 1 mark lost).
> 2. **Forgetting the iterative nature of the methodology** — Writing the three risk-reduction steps as a list, not a loop, loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up units in the safety distance formula** — Using $K = 2$ m/s instead of 2,000 mm/s gives the same numerical answer only if you also convert $T$ to seconds. Examiners watch for unit consistency.
> 4. **Confusing PL (Performance Level) with SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** — They are similar but not identical; PL is a KTU/ISO construct, SIL comes from IEC 61508. Mapping without context loses marks.
> 5. **Omitting documentation in the answer** — Risk assessment is **mandated** to be documented in a **Technical File** (Annex I, Machinery Directive). Forgetting this costs ½ to 1 mark.
> 6. **Stating "zero risk" as a goal** — Risk can never be zero; the correct phrasing is **"acceptable residual risk"**. Writing "zero risk" is technically wrong.
> 7. **Skipping the iteration** — After suggesting a safeguard, the student should explicitly say *"Re-estimate residual risk and continue the loop until acceptable."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Safety of Machinery**

- [x] The governing standard is **ISO 12100:2010**; in the EU it is enforced through the **Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC**.
- [x] The **risk-reduction methodology is iterative** (3 steps): inherent safe design → safeguarding → information for use.
- [x] **Type A** standards are basic (e.g., ISO 12100). **Type B1** are general (ISO 13849-1). **Type B2** are specific (ISO 13851). **Type C** are machine-specific (ISO 10218).
- [x] **Type C overrides Type B overrides Type A.**
- [x] **Performance Level (PL)** ranges from **a (lowest)** to **e (highest)**.
- [x] **Categories** B, 1, 2, 3, 4 describe **architecture**, not reliability.
- [x] **PL is a function of: Category + DC + MTTFd + CCF** (refer to bar chart, Annex E).
- [x] **DC** = Low (60–90%), Medium (90–99%), High (≥ 99%).
- [x] **MTTFd** = Low (< 3 yr), Medium (3–10 yr), High (10–100 yr).
- [x] **CCF** score must be **≥ 65/100** to be acceptable.
- [x] **Safety distance formula:** $S = (K \times T) + C$ where $K = 2{,}000$ mm/s (hand) or $1{,}600$ mm/s (body).
- [x] **C = 0** for finger detection (resolution ≤ 14 mm); for whole-body detection $C \ge 850$ mm.
- [x] **Stop Categories (EN 60204-1):** 0 = uncontrolled power removal; 1 = controlled stop + power removal; 2 = controlled stop + power retained (requires additional safety monitoring).
- [x] **Emergency stop (ISO 13850)** must be **Category 0 or 1** stop, **red mushroom-head button on yellow background**, self-latching, and must **override all other commands**.
- [x] **Two-hand control (ISO 13851)** requires **simultaneous actuation within 0.5 s** and **anti-tie-down** to defeat bypass.
- [x] **Risk can never be zero** — only **acceptably low**.
- [x] **CE marking** requires: Technical File + Declaration of Conformity + Risk Assessment + Operating Manual.
- [x] **Indian context:** Factories Act 1948 (Sections 21, 22), OSH Code 2020, BIS standards, and the **National Board of Boiler and Industrial Safety** for pressure systems.
- [x] **Most cited exam keywords:** *risk graph*, *PLr*, *SFF*, *DC*, *MTTFd*, *CCF*, *PFHd*, *bar chart*, *iterative loop*, *residual risk*, *Technical File*, *CE Marking*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When in doubt in the examination hall, **always cite a standard number and its year** (e.g., *“as per EN ISO 13849-1:2015, Category 3…”*). This single habit typically lifts a 7-mark answer to a full-mark answer because the KTU valuation key explicitly awards marks for **correct standard citation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Criteria and Related Test Methods for Service Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT417 - ROBOT SAFETY AND STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Criteria and Related Test Methods for Service Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Performance Criteria and Related Test Methods for Service Robots** refers to the standardized set of measurable specifications, operational benchmarks, and verification protocols used to objectively evaluate whether a service robot satisfies its intended functional, safety, and reliability requirements as defined under international standards (primarily **ISO 13482:2014** for personal care robots, supplemented by **ISO 10218**, **ISO/TS 15066**, and **ISO 8373**).

A **service robot** is formally defined (per ISO 8373) as a robot that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, excluding industrial automation applications. Performance criteria specify the *quantitative thresholds* (e.g., maximum velocity, stopping distance, force limits), while test methods prescribe the *reproducible procedures* used to verify those thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The 2024 KTU scheme explicitly emphasizes three pillars: (1) **Performance metrics** (accuracy, repeatability, payload, speed, autonomy duration), (2) **Safety-related test methods** (electrical, mechanical, environmental, EMC), and (3) **Verification & Validation (V&V)** procedures aligned with the type test philosophy of IEC/ISO.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Test" for Robots

Think of performance criteria and test methods like a **driving license test for a self-driving car**:

- **Performance criteria** = the rules of the road (must stop within 5 m at 30 km/h, must detect a pedestrian at 30 m, etc.).
- **Test methods** = the actual driving exam maneuvers (parallel parking test, emergency brake test, vision test).
- **Acceptance** = the test certificate (Type Test approval per ISO).

Just as a driving school cannot claim a car is "safe" by intuition, a robot manufacturer cannot claim a service robot is "safe and performant" without passing a documented, repeatable, and standards-based test.

> [!NOTE]
> The three categories of **Personal Care Robots** (defined in ISO 13482) are:
> 1. **Mobile Servant Robot** — performs servicing tasks (e.g., cleaning, fetch-and-carry).
> 2. **Physical Assistant Robot** — provides physical assistance to a person (e.g., exoskeleton, patient lift).
> 3. **Person Carrier Robot** — transports a person (e.g., autonomous wheelchair).

### 1.3 Performance vs. Safety — The Two Sides of the Same Coin

| Aspect | Performance Criteria | Safety Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Ensure robot does its *job well* | Ensure robot *does not harm* users/environment |
| **Measured by** | Productivity, accuracy, uptime | Forces, energies, contact pressures |
| **Test Type** | Functional / benchmark tests | Type tests, fault condition tests |
| **Standard** | ISO 9283 (manipulating), ISO 18646 (mobile) | ISO 13482, ISO 13849, IEC 61508 |
| **Failure consequence** | Poor user experience | Injury, death, legal liability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** Examiners often ask students to *distinguish* between performance and safety test methods. Memorize: *Performance = "Can the robot do what it claims to do?"* and *Safety = "Will the robot hurt someone while doing it?"*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Performance Testing

Performance testing for service robots follows a layered architecture, moving from **low-level component tests** up to **system-level mission tests**:

1. **Component Level (L1)** — Individual sensors, actuators, batteries, controllers.
2. **Subsystem Level (L2)** — Drive train, navigation stack, vision pipeline, gripper mechanism.
3. **System Level (L3)** — Full robot performing representative service tasks in a realistic environment.
4. **Mission Level (L4)** — Long-duration endurance, multi-day deployment, fault recovery.

At each level, the test method must define:
- **Pre-conditions** (environment, robot state, charge level).
- **Stimuli / Inputs** (commands, obstacles, payloads).
- **Measurement points** (sensors, observers, instrumentation).
- **Pass/Fail criteria** (numerical thresholds from the standard).
- **Test report contents** (per ISO/IEC 17025 general requirements).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Performance Metric

| Metric | Why It Matters | Practical Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Pose Accuracy (AP)** | Robot must reach the *correct* location | Determines success of pick-and-place, navigation |
| **Pose Repeatability (RP)** | Robot must reach the *same* location consistently | Critical for safety stops, docking, handover tasks |
| **Path Accuracy (AT)** | Robot must follow a *specific* trajectory | Important for medical assistance, human-coexistence paths |
| **Path Repeatability (RT)** | Robot must repeat the *same* trajectory | Ensures predictable behavior for the human user |
| **Pose Stabilization Time** | Robot must *settle* within a deadline | Affects human-robot interaction latency |
| **Drift Rate** | Localization error per unit time | Critical for long-duration autonomous service |
| **Maximum Safe Velocity (v_max)** | Kinetic energy must be limited | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ directly governs injury severity |
| **Stopping Distance (d_s)** | Emergency stop must be fast enough | $d_s = v_0 \cdot t_{react} + \tfrac{v_0^2}{2a_{brake}}$ |
| **Battery Endurance (E_b)** | Service must be uninterrupted | $E_b = V \cdot I \cdot t_{discharge}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A hospital deploying an autonomous pharmacy robot cannot accept *pose repeatability* worse than **±2 mm** at the gripper, otherwise medication vials will be missed. Likewise, the **maximum safe velocity** of a person carrier robot in a corridor must be limited so that a collision cannot deliver more than the biomechanical limit defined in **ISO/TS 15066** (quasi-static contact: $F_{max} = 50$ N for the chest, transient contact: $E_{max} = 0.5$ J).

### 2.3 The Three Categories of Test Methods (ISO 13482 / IEC Framework)

**Category 1 — Type Tests (Design Validation):**
Performed on a representative production unit to verify that the design meets all requirements. Done **once** per design (or design change). Examples: EMC immunity, drop test, IP rating, climatic chamber test.

**Category 2 — Routine Tests (Production Validation):**
Performed on **every** manufactured unit. Examples: ground continuity, insulation resistance, functional self-test on power-up, brake operation check.

**Category 3 — Acceptance Tests (Site Validation):**
Performed at the customer's site after installation. Examples: site-specific risk assessment, integration with other equipment, training verification, emergency stop response time on the actual floor.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Domain / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_k$ | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J (Joule) | Kinetic energy of moving robot link |
| $d_s$ | $d_s = v_0 t_r + \tfrac{v_0^2}{2 a_b}$ | m | Stopping distance (kinematic) |
| $F_{max}$ | $F_{max} = m a_{max}$ | N | Max transient contact force |
| $P_{max}$ | $P_{max} = F_{max} \cdot v_{contact}$ | W | Max contact power (ISO/TS 15066) |
| $E_{contact}$ | $E_{contact} = \int F \cdot dx$ | J | Contact energy (transient) |
| $AP$ | $AP = \bar{\Delta x_p}$ | mm | Pose accuracy (mean error) |
| $RP$ | $RP = \bar{\Delta x_r} + 3\sigma_r$ | mm | Pose repeatability (ISO 9283) |
| $MTBF$ | $MTBF = \tfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | h | Mean Time Between Failures |
| $MTTR$ | $MTTR = \tfrac{\sum t_{repair}}{N_{repairs}}$ | h | Mean Time To Repair |
| $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | h$^{-1}$ | Failure rate |
| Availability $A$ | $A = \tfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | System availability (0 to 1) |
| $PFH_d$ | $PFH_d = \lambda_D \cdot t_{cycle}$ | h$^{-1}$ | Probability of dangerous failure per hour |
| $PL$ | $PL = a + b + c$ (risk graph) | a–e | Performance Level (ISO 13849-1) |
| $SIL$ | $SIL = f(Hazard_{severity}, Probability)$ | 1–4 | Safety Integrity Level (IEC 61508) |
| $v_{safe}$ | $v_{safe} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2 \cdot E_{max}}{m_{eff}}}$ | m/s | Maximum safe velocity for energy limit |
| $t_{stop}$ | $t_{stop} = t_{detect} + t_{react} + t_{brake}$ | s | Total emergency stop time |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule (KR-Board):** Always write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never use the raw pipe `|` character inside a table cell or it will break the column layout during PDF rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

In a **hospital pharmacy delivery robot**, the engineering team must verify:
- **Pose accuracy** at the dispensary drawer: $\le 5$ mm (performance).
- **Maximum speed in corridors**: $0.7$ m/s so that $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}(60)(0.7)^2 = 14.7$ J, which exceeds the **0.5 J** transient contact limit — therefore either speed must be reduced to $v \le 0.13$ m/s, **or** a soft compliant bumper must be added, **or** force-sensing skin with active stop must be installed.
- **EMC immunity** to a defibrillator's $50$ kV pulse, per **IEC 60601-1-2**.
- **Biocompatibility** of any surface contacting skin (glove compartment, hand-hold), per **ISO 10993**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Safe Velocity from Energy Limit

The biomechanical threshold for **transient contact** (ISO/TS 15066) limits the contact energy to a maximum value $E_{max}$ (typically $0.5$ J for the chest region). We derive the maximum permissible velocity of a moving robot link of effective mass $m_{eff}$.

**Step 1 — State the constraint.** The kinetic energy at impact must not exceed the biomechanical limit:
$$E_k \le E_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the kinetic energy formula.** With $m = m_{eff}$ and $v = v_{impact}$:
$$\frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v_{impact}^{2} \le E_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the velocity term.** Multiply both sides by $2 / m_{eff}$:
$$v_{impact}^{2} \le \frac{2 E_{max}}{m_{eff}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the positive square root** (velocity is non-negative):
$$v_{impact} \le \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{max}}{m_{eff}}}$$

**Step 5 — State the engineering result.** The **maximum safe impact velocity** is therefore:
$$v_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{max}}{m_{eff}}}$$

**Reasoning (Why this matters):** This equation directly couples the *mechanical design* (mass of the link, choice of materials) with the *safety performance* (maximum allowed speed). A heavy robot cannot move as fast as a light one in human-shared spaces, *unless* the energy is dissipated by compliant padding (changing the effective contact dynamics, requiring re-derivation using the impulse-momentum theorem).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Emergency Stopping Distance

For a service robot travelling at initial velocity $v_0$ that issues an emergency stop, the total stopping distance consists of a *reaction phase* (constant velocity) and a *braking phase* (constant deceleration).

**Step 1 — Define the reaction distance.** During the reaction time $t_r$ (time between the stop command and the brakes engaging), the robot travels at constant $v_0$:
$$d_{react} = v_0 \cdot t_r$$

**Step 2 — Define the braking distance.** Assuming constant deceleration $a_b$ (positive scalar), the kinematic equation $v_f^2 = v_0^2 - 2 a_b d_{brake}$ is used. Setting final velocity $v_f = 0$:
$$0 = v_0^2 - 2 a_b d_{brake}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for braking distance:**
$$d_{brake} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_b}$$

**Step 4 — Total stopping distance:**
$$d_{stop} = d_{react} + d_{brake} = v_0 t_r + \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_b}$$

**Step 5 — Total stopping time** (using $t = v_0 / a_b$ for the braking phase):
$$t_{stop} = t_r + \frac{v_0}{a_b}$$

**Reasoning (Why this matters):** ISO 13482 mandates that the safety-rated reduced speed and stop functions are verifiable. This equation is the *numerical backbone* of the safety verification. If the robot's controller guarantees $a_b \ge 2.0$ m/s$^2$ and the perception stack has $t_r \le 0.15$ s, then at $v_0 = 1.0$ m/s the stopping distance is $d_{stop} = (1.0)(0.15) + (1.0^2)/(2 \cdot 2.0) = 0.15 + 0.25 = 0.40$ m.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: System Availability

A service robot deployed in a 24/7 hospital must be *available* (not undergoing repair) for a target fraction of the time.

**Step 1 — Define MTBF and MTTR.**
$$MTBF = \frac{T_{total\_operating}}{N_{failures}}, \quad MTTR = \frac{\sum t_{repairs}}{N_{repairs}}$$

**Step 2 — In one operational cycle,** the robot runs for $MTBF$ hours, then takes $MTTR$ hours to repair. The cycle time is:
$$T_{cycle} = MTBF + MTTR$$

**Step 3 — Availability** is the fraction of time the robot is operational:
$$A = \frac{MTBF}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** Suppose field data gives $MTBF = 800$ h and $MTTR = 2$ h:
$$A = \frac{800}{800 + 2} = \frac{800}{802} = 0.9975 \quad (\text{i.e., } 99.75\%)$$

**Reasoning (Why this matters):** A service-level agreement (SLA) typically demands $A \ge 0.99$. If the calculated availability is below target, the design must either (a) increase $MTBF$ (better components, redundancy) or (b) decrease $MTTR$ (modular design, hot-swap batteries, remote diagnostics).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Test Method → Performance Parameter → Pass Criterion

| Test Method | Performance / Safety Parameter Measured | Pass Criterion (Typical) | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pose repeatability test | $RP$ | $\le \pm 2$ mm (medical) | ISO 9283 |
| Path accuracy test | $AT$ | $\le \pm 5$ mm (cleaning) | ISO 9283 |
| Maximum velocity test | $v_{max}$ | $v \le v_{safe}$ (energy-limited) | ISO 13482 §5.10 |
| Stopping distance test | $d_{stop}$ | $\le 0.3$ m at $v_0 = 1.0$ m/s | ISO 13482 §5.11 |
| EMC immunity test | Susceptibility to RF | Criterion A (no functional change) | IEC 61000-4-3 |
| Climatic chamber test | Operating temperature range | $-10^{\circ}$C to $+40^{\circ}$C functional | IEC 60068-2-1/2 |
| Drop test (packaged) | Packaging integrity | No functional damage after 1.0 m drop | IEC 60068-2-31 |
| IP rating test | Ingress protection | IP54 (dust + splashes) for indoor | IEC 60529 |
| Acoustic noise test | Sound pressure level | $\le 65$ dB(A) at 1 m | ISO 11201 |
| Insulation resistance | Dielectric strength | $\ge 1$ M$\Omega$ at 500 V DC | IEC 60335-1 |
| Ground continuity | Earth bond resistance | $\le 0.1$ $\Omega$ | IEC 60335-1 |
| Endurance cycle test | Cycle count to failure | $\ge 50{,}000$ cycles | ISO 13849 (B10d) |
| Collision force test | Max contact force | $F \le 50$ N (quasi-static chest) | ISO/TS 15066 |
| Battery abuse test | Thermal runaway | No fire / explosion | UN 38.3, IEC 62133 |
| Biocompatibility test | Cytotoxicity, sensitization | Pass per ISO 10993 | ISO 13482 §5.7 |
| Software unit test | Code coverage | $\ge 80\%$ statement coverage | IEC 61508-3 |
| Long-duration mission | Mission success rate | $\ge 95\%$ over 100 missions | Custom (service spec) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive)

**Problem:** A mobile servant robot weighs **$m = 45$ kg** and operates in a home environment. The maximum allowed transient contact energy on the chest is **$E_{max} = 0.5$ J** (ISO/TS 15066). The braking deceleration is **$a_b = 1.5$ m/s$^2$** and the reaction time is **$t_r = 0.2$ s**. Find: (a) maximum safe velocity, (b) total stopping distance at this velocity, (c) total stopping time.

**Solution:**

**(a) Maximum safe velocity:**
$$v_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{max}}{m_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.5}{45}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{45}} = \sqrt{0.0222}$$
$$v_{safe} \approx 0.149 \, \text{m/s} \approx 0.54 \, \text{km/h}$$

**(b) Total stopping distance at $v_0 = v_{safe}$:**
$$d_{react} = v_0 \cdot t_r = 0.149 \times 0.2 = 0.0298 \, \text{m}$$
$$d_{brake} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_b} = \frac{(0.149)^2}{2 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.0222}{3.0} = 0.0074 \, \text{m}$$
$$d_{stop} = 0.0298 + 0.0074 = 0.0372 \, \text{m} \approx 37 \, \text{mm}$$

**(c) Total stopping time:**
$$t_{stop} = t_r + \frac{v_0}{a_b} = 0.2 + \frac{0.149}{1.5} = 0.2 + 0.099 = 0.299 \, \text{s} \approx 0.3 \, \text{s}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The robot must cruise at very low speed ($< 0.54$ km/h) to stay within the 0.5 J limit. In practice, designers combine this with a *compliant bumper* that increases the stopping time and reduces peak force, *or* install force-sensing skin that triggers a faster, smarter stop, *or* restrict the robot's operating area away from chest-height contact zones.

The following diagram maps the physical test setup used to verify the stopping distance of a service robot, in accordance with the test method derived in Section 3.2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    ESTOP --> Robot
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (KR-Board):** Each block corresponds to a **test instrument** in the laboratory. The **time-synchronized trigger** is critical — without it, the camera, encoders, and IMU will record slightly different event times, causing errors of 10–50 ms that can invalidate a sub-100 mm stopping distance measurement. This is a common practical pitfall noted by ISO 13482 working groups.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Type Test" as applied to service robots. How does it differ from a "Routine Test"? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Type Test** is a conformity test performed on a representative sample of a production batch to verify that the design of the robot meets all the safety and performance requirements of the applicable standard (e.g., ISO 13482, IEC 60335-1). It is carried out **once per design or design change**. Example: EMC immunity test in a reverberation chamber.

A **Routine Test** is a test performed on **every individual unit** leaving the production line to confirm that the manufactured robot is free from manufacturing defects. Example: Ground continuity check (earth bond resistance $\le 0.1$ $\Omega$) on every unit before shipment.

| Aspect | Type Test | Routine Test |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Once per design | Every unit |
| Purpose | Design validation | Production validation |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Duration | Days/weeks | Minutes |

*(1.5 marks for definitions + 1 mark for examples + 0.5 mark for differentiation table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain why pose repeatability is more critical than pose accuracy for a service robot performing a handover task with a human user.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pose accuracy (AP)** measures how close the robot's *average* achieved pose is to the *commanded* pose — it is a *systematic* error. **Pose repeatability (RP)** measures the *variation* in achieved pose when the same command is given repeatedly — it is a *random* error.

In a **handover task**, the human user adapts their hand position to the robot's *actual* end-effector location. They do not need the robot to be at the *exact* commanded position; they need the robot to be at the **same** position every time so that they can predict where to place their hand. If the robot is consistently 10 mm to the right (inaccurate but repeatable), the user simply moves 10 mm to the right. But if the robot scatters randomly within a ±15 mm cloud, the user cannot predict the handover point, leading to failed handovers, frustration, and potential pinching injuries.

Therefore, **repeatability is more critical** for predictable, safe human-robot interaction. ISO 13482 references ISO 9283 for these metrics, and most service-robot specifications quote $RP$ (not $AP$) as the headline figure.

*(1 mark accuracy vs. repeatability distinction + 1 mark handover scenario reasoning + 1 mark ISO/standard reference.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With neat schematics and equations, derive the relationship between the **maximum safe velocity** of a service robot link and the **biomechanical contact energy limit** specified in ISO/TS 15066. State all assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mobile servant robot of mass **$m = 60$ kg** operates in an elderly care home. The maximum permitted transient contact energy on the chest is **$E_{max} = 0.5$ J**. The braking deceleration is **$a_b = 2.0$ m/s$^2$** and the perception-to-brake reaction time is **$t_r = 0.18$ s**. Calculate: (i) the maximum safe velocity, (ii) the total stopping distance at that velocity, and (iii) the total stopping time. State whether the robot can be safely operated at **$v_0 = 0.2$ m/s** in corridors shared with elderly residents. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

We define:
- $m_{eff}$ = effective mass of the moving robot link (kg).
- $v_{impact}$ = velocity of the link at the moment of contact (m/s).
- $E_{max}$ = maximum permissible transient contact energy (J), from ISO/TS 15066.

**Step 1 — Energy constraint.** From the biomechanical limit, the kinetic energy at impact must satisfy:
$$E_k \le E_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy expression.** For a rigid link of effective mass $m_{eff}$ moving at $v_{impact}$:
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v_{impact}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two equations:**
$$\frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v_{impact}^{2} \le E_{max}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_{impact}$:**
$$v_{impact}^{2} \le \frac{2 E_{max}}{m_{eff}}$$

$$v_{impact} \le \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{max}}{m_{eff}}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.** The maximum safe velocity scales with the square root of $E_{max}$ and inversely with the square root of the effective mass. This means:
- Doubling the energy limit increases $v_{safe}$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
- Quadrupling the mass halves the safe velocity.
- Therefore, designers either **reduce mass**, **reduce speed**, or **add compliance** (which changes the impact dynamics, requiring impulse-based analysis).

**Assumptions explicitly stated:**
1. The link is treated as a **rigid body** with all mass concentrated at the center of percussion.
2. The contact is **inelastic** (no rebound), so all kinetic energy is transferred.
3. No **active force control** or **compliant covering** is present.
4. The collision direction is **perpendicular** to the chest surface (worst case).
5. ISO/TS 15066 quasi-static and transient limits are applicable (no sharp edges).

*(Schematic: a simple free-body diagram showing a robot link of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ toward a human chest, with the impact point marked. Marks: 1 for energy constraint, 2 for kinetic energy formula, 2 for algebraic derivation, 1 for assumptions, 1 for engineering interpretation.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**(i) Maximum safe velocity:**
$$v_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{max}}{m_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.5}{60}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{60}} = \sqrt{0.01667}$$
$$v_{safe} \approx 0.129 \, \text{m/s} \quad (\text{approx. } 0.46 \, \text{km/h})$$

**(ii) Total stopping distance at $v_0 = v_{safe}$:**
$$d_{react} = v_0 \cdot t_r = 0.129 \times 0.18 = 0.02322 \, \text{m}$$
$$d_{brake} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_b} = \frac{(0.129)^2}{2 \times 2.0} = \frac{0.01664}{4.0} = 0.00416 \, \text{m}$$
$$d_{stop} = d_{react} + d_{brake} = 0.02322 + 0.00416 = 0.02738 \, \text{m} \approx 27.4 \, \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Total stopping time:**
$$t_{stop} = t_r + \frac{v_0}{a_b} = 0.18 + \frac{0.129}{2.0} = 0.18 + 0.0645 = 0.2445 \, \text{s} \approx 0.245 \, \text{s}$$

**Safety verdict for $v_0 = 0.2$ m/s:**

Compare the impact energy at $v_0 = 0.2$ m/s to the limit:
$$E_{impact} = \frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 60 \times (0.2)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 60 \times 0.04 = 1.2 \, \text{J}$$

Since $E_{impact} = 1.2$ J $\gg E_{max} = 0.5$ J, **the robot is NOT safe** to operate at $0.2$ m/s in corridors shared with elderly residents under rigid-body assumption. The impact energy is **2.4× above the limit**.

**Remedial options:**
- **Reduce speed** to $v_{safe} \approx 0.129$ m/s (very slow, impractical).
- **Add a compliant bumper** that increases contact time and reduces peak force (changing the rigid-body assumption).
- **Install force-sensing skin** that triggers a faster, pre-emptive stop.
- **Restrict the operating area** to rooms without direct resident contact.

*(Marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 2 for (iii), 1 for energy comparison.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define **Performance Criteria** and **Test Methods** for service robots. Explain the **three categories of personal care robots** defined in ISO 13482 and give one example application for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe in detail the **pose repeatability test** as specified in ISO 9283. Include the test setup, the measurement procedure, the data processing steps, and the formula used to compute $RP$. Why is the **3-sigma** factor used in the repeatability formula? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Performance Criteria** are the quantitative specifications that a service robot must meet to fulfill its intended function. They include:
- **Pose accuracy** ($AP$) and **pose repeatability** ($RP$).
- **Path accuracy** ($AT$) and **path repeatability** ($RT$).
- **Maximum payload**, **maximum reach**, **maximum speed**.
- **Battery endurance**, **pose stabilization time**, **autonomy duration**.
- **Stopping distance** under emergency stop.

**Test Methods** are the standardized, reproducible procedures used to measure whether the performance criteria are met. They specify the test setup, the number of trials (typically 30 per ISO 9283), the data acquisition, and the post-processing formulas.

**Three categories of personal care robots (ISO 13482):**

| Category | Description | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobile Servant Robot** | A robot with a mobile base that performs service tasks in a human environment. | A cleaning robot (e.g., autonomous vacuum cleaner) or a hospital pharmacy delivery robot. |
| **Physical Assistant Robot** | A robot (often wearable or co-located) that assists a person with physical tasks by providing supplementary force. | A lower-limb exoskeleton for assisted walking, or a robotic patient lift for caregivers. |
| **Person Carrier Robot** | A robot designed to transport a person from one location to another. | An autonomous wheelchair, a mobility scooter with collaborative control, or a hospital transporter. |

Each category has its own hazard profile, and the standard's clauses 5.x are organized around these three categories.

*(Marks: 1 for performance criteria, 1 for test methods, 3 for the three categories with examples, 2 for clarity and standard references.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Test Setup (ISO 9283 §6.2):**
- A **measurement cube** is defined within the robot's workspace (typically 30 points distributed across the cube).
- A **fiducial marker** (precision target) is fixed to the robot's end-effector.
- A **metrology system** (laser tracker, coordinate measuring machine, or photogrammetry cameras) is used to record the 3D position of the target.

**Measurement Procedure:**
1. Command the robot to move to pose $P_1$ (one of the 30 cube points).
2. Allow the robot to settle (hold for a defined stabilization time, typically 1–3 seconds).
3. Record 3D position $x_{i,j,k}$ where $i$ = cube point, $j$ = trial number, $k$ = coordinate axis $(x, y, z)$.
4. Repeat steps 1–3 for all 30 cube points.
5. Repeat the entire cycle **30 times** to gather statistical data.

**Data Processing:**
- For each cube point $i$, compute the **mean position** $\bar{x}_i$ over the 30 trials.
- For each cube point $i$, compute the **standard deviation** $\sigma_i$ over the 30 trials.
- The **pose repeatability** $RP$ is defined as:
$$RP = \bar{l} + 3 S_l$$
where $\bar{l}$ is the mean of the standard deviations $\sigma_i$ across all 30 points, and $S_l$ is the standard deviation of those standard deviations (a measure of how uniform the repeatability is across the workspace).

**Why the 3-sigma factor?**

In a **Gaussian (normal) distribution** of measurement errors, the property is that **99.73%** of all measurements fall within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean. The 3-sigma factor therefore provides a **statistical upper bound** on the random error — it is the engineering convention (used in both ISO 9283 and ISO 5725) for expressing a "worst-case expected deviation" without using the absolute maximum. The value $3\sigma$ is a trade-off: it captures nearly all the actual measurements (less than 0.27% lie outside) while remaining a practical, single-number summary of the spread.

**Engineering interpretation:** A robot with $RP = \pm 0.5$ mm (with 3-sigma) means that during normal operation, the end-effector will lie within a 0.5 mm radius sphere of the commanded position, **99.73% of the time**.

*(Marks: 2 for setup, 2 for procedure, 1 for formula, 1 for the 3-sigma reasoning, 1 for engineering interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "accuracy" and "repeatability"** — A robot can be *inaccurate* but *highly repeatable* (systematic offset). Board examiners specifically check whether the student understands this distinction. Always quote both $AP$ and $RP$ with correct units.
> 2. **Mixing ISO 9283 (industrial manipulators) with ISO 13482 (personal care robots)** — They are *related* but *not identical*. ISO 9283 gives the *test method* for pose metrics; ISO 13482 *references* ISO 9283 but adds service-specific safety requirements. Citing the wrong standard is a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the 3-sigma factor** in the $RP$ formula. Writing $RP = \bar{l}$ alone (without the $3 S_l$ term) is incomplete.
> 4. **Wrong unit of stopping distance** — must be in **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)**, not "cm".
> 5. **No assumptions stated** in derivations — the examiner expects at least 2–3 explicit assumptions (rigid body, no compliance, perpendicular contact, etc.). Skipping this loses 1 mark consistently.
> 6. **Energy vs. Force confusion** — ISO/TS 15066 specifies both **quasi-static force limits** (e.g., 50 N chest) and **transient energy limits** (e.g., 0.5 J). Students often quote force when energy is asked (or vice versa).
> 7. **Skipping the engineering interpretation** at the end of numerical problems — KTU's outcome-based education (OBE) policy requires the *significance* of the numerical answer to be discussed. A correct number with no interpretation is worth only 80% of the marks allotted.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Three categories of personal care robots** (per ISO 13482): **Mobile Servant**, **Physical Assistant**, **Person Carrier**.
- [ ] **ISO 13482** is the master standard for personal care robots; **ISO 9283** provides the test methods for pose/path metrics.
- [ ] **Three test categories:** Type Test (design), Routine Test (production), Acceptance Test (site).
- [ ] **Pose accuracy (AP)** = closeness of *mean* achieved pose to commanded pose.
- [ ] **Pose repeatability (RP)** = closeness of *consecutive* achieved poses to each other; formula is $RP = \bar{l} + 3 S_l$ with the **3-sigma** factor.
- [ ] **Path accuracy / repeatability** are *trajectory* analogues of pose accuracy / repeatability.
- [ ] **Maximum safe velocity** is derived from the **kinetic energy** constraint: $v_{safe} = \sqrt{2 E_{max} / m_{eff}}$.
- [ ] **Biomechanical limits** (ISO/TS 15066): chest quasi-static force $\le 50$ N, transient contact energy $\le 0.5$ J.
- [ ] **Stopping distance** = $d_{stop} = v_0 t_r + v_0^2 / (2 a_b)$, with **two phases**: reaction (constant velocity) and braking (constant deceleration).
- [ ] **Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$; typical SLA target $\ge 0.99$.
- [ ] **MTBF** = total operating time / number of failures; **MTTR** = total repair time / number of repairs.
- [ ] **PFH_d** (probability of dangerous failure per hour) is the key safety metric; related to **PL** (Performance Level, ISO 13849) and **SIL** (Safety Integrity Level, IEC 61508).
- [ ] **EMC test standards**: IEC 61000-4-2 (ESD), IEC 61000-4-3 (radiated RF), IEC 61000-4-4 (burst), IEC 61000-4-5 (surge).
- [ ] **Ingress Protection (IP)** rating per IEC 60529 — e.g., IP54 = dust-protected + splash-resistant.
- [ ] **Climatic tests** per IEC 60068-2 — operating temperature typically $-10^{\circ}$C to $+40^{\circ}$C for indoor service robots.
- [ ] **Biocompatibility** per ISO 10993 for any robot surface that may contact skin (grippers, arm rests, handles).
- [ ] **Battery safety** per UN 38.3 (transport) and IEC 62133 (operation) — short-circuit, overcharge, thermal abuse, drop, vibration, pressure tests.
- [ ] **Software V&V** per IEC 61508-3 — code coverage, modular testing, integration testing, hazard analysis.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** in derivations: rigid body, inelastic collision, perpendicular contact, no compliance, worst-case loading.
- [ ] **Always provide an engineering interpretation** of numerical answers (OBE requirement, KTU 2024 scheme).
- [ ] **Never fabricate textbook references** — use only ISO, IEC, and KTU syllabus documents.
- [ ] **Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in tables**, never the raw pipe `|` character (it breaks the markdown table in PDF rendering).
